text: EMSWORTH, a chapelry and seaport in the p. of Warblington, Hants, 2 m. SE of Havant, on the London, Brighton, and Portsmouth railway, by which it is 36 m. from Brighton, and 9 m. from Portsmouth. Pop. 1,165, many of whom are engaged in ship-building and in rope-making.
EMU-BAY, a confined anchorage on the coast of Australia, in S lat. 41° 2', W long. of Sydney 5° 18' 50". A river of the same name runs into it.
EMU-FORD, a village of E. Australia, in Westmoreland co., on the Hawkesbury river, 12 m. above Wilberforce.

EMU-PLAINS, a district in Westmoreland co., in E. Australia, stretching along the Hawkesbury river, and comprising several fertile valleys to the W of the Blue mountains.—The same name has been given to a portion of country on Victoria river, in N. Australia, to the E of Wickham heights, and intersected by the parallel of 15° 34' S.
EMYVALE, a village in the p. of Donagh, co. Monaghan, 5 m. S of Aughnacloy. Pop. 694.
ENA, a river of Sweden, which runs into Lake Storsjore, in the prov. of Jämtland, after an E, WNW, and ESE course of about 80 m.
ENAMAS-DAGH, a mountain-ridge of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, detached from the main chain of Taurus, in about 37° 20' N lat., and running N and NW towards the Murad-Dagh.
ENAMERA, a village of Abyssinia, 120 m. SSW of Gondar.

ENARA, an extensive lake of Russian Lapland, in the duchy of Finland, in N lat. 69°. It is 40 m. long, and 16 m. broad; is full of small islands, and communicates with the Arctic sea by means of a river called the Pasvig.—Also a town on the W side of the lake of the same name, 80 m. SW of Wardhuys. A fair is held here in the beginning of November, to which traders repair to purchase deerskins, stock-fish, and furs.

ENAREA, or NAREA, a district to the SW of the Abyssinian territory of Shoa, between the parallels of 7° and 8° N. Its cap. is Saka. It is inhabited by Gallas, who export coffee, grain, cattle, ivory, and slaves, to Abyssinia. It seems to be an elevated plain, watered by several rivers, and deriving from its lofty position the comfort of a temperate climate.
ENBORNE, a parish in Berks, 2 m. WSW of Newbury. Area 2,550 acres. Pop. in 1801, 275; in 1841, 384.
ENCABELLADOS, a tribe of Indians in the Brazilian prov. of Para, scattered along various tributaries of the Amazon.
ENCAMP, a village in the valley of Andorra, on the r. bank of the Embalire, 3 m. N of Andorre-la- Vieille.
ENCANTADA, a lake of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, which discharges itself into the Paraguaçu.— Also a lake in the prov. of Piauhy, connected with the Parnahiba river.
ENCARNACION (LA), an island in the S. Pacific ocean, discovered by Quiros in 1606. It is in S lat. 24° 45′.
ENCARTACIONES (Las), a mountainous district of Spain, in the W part of Biscay.
ENCHENREUTH, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, 4 m. NE of Kupferberg. Pop. 500.
ENCINA (LA), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Salamanca, 7 m. S of Ciudad-Rodrigo, near the r. bank of the Agueda.
ENCINA-CORBA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 20 m. ESE of Calatayud.
ENCINA-SOLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 60 m. S of Badajoz, on the r. bank of the Murtiga.
ENCINAS-DE-ESGUEVA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. ENE of Valladolid, on the l. bank of the Esgueva.
ENCISO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Logrono, on the l. bank of the Cidacos.
ENCKHUYSEN. See ENKHUISEN.
ENCOCHE (San-Jose-de), a Portuguese fort in the Congo territory of Lower Guinea, 200 m. SSE of San Salvador, on the Baca river.

ENCOUNTER-BAY, an inlet of the coast of S. Australia, in S lat. 35° 30', E long. 139°. There is good anchorage for small vessels inside an island which lies off this bay; but this anchorage is not safe for more than 5 months in the year.
ENCOUNTER (POINT), a headland on the coast of Arctic America, in N lat. 69° 16', W long. 136° 20'.
ENDAPILLY, a town of India, in the circar of Rajamundri, in N lat. 16° 58'.
ENDAPUR, a town of India, in the prov. of Mysore, in N lat. 13° 50'. END
ENDAVA, a river of New Grenada, which flows into the Orinoco, on the l. bank, a little above the mouth of the Meta, after a course of about 60 m. from W to E.
ENDE, a port on the S coast of the isle of Flores, in S lat. 8° 52', E long. 121° 42'.
ENDEAVOUR-HILL, a peaked isolated hill near the entrance of Victoria river, in N. Australia, about 1 m. S of Blunder-bay. Alt. 690 ft.

ENDEAVOUR-RIVER, a river on the N coast of Australia, which flows into the ocean in about 15° 27' 4" S lat., E long. 145° 10' 49". [King.] At low water there is not above 9 or 10 ft. water on the bar, and not above 17 or 18 ft. at full tide. Turtle is to be obtained here, but animals on the shore are not numerous, nor is the soil productive.

ENDEAVOUR-STRAIT, the channel lying between the peninsular projection of Cape York, on the E side of the gulf of Carpentaria, in Australia, and Wolf's and Cook's islands, in S lat. 10° 45'. The coast of the main is here level and of moderate elevation, and subsides rapidly to the SW, where it scarcely reaches an elevation of 50 ft. The opposite or N side of the strait, formed by the largest of the Prince of Wales's group, is high and rocky.
ENDEAVOUR STRAITS. See Torres STRAITS.
ENDELAVE, a small island of Denmark, in the Cattegat, belonging to the bail. of Aarhuus, in N lat. 55° 47'.
ENDELLION, a parish of Cornwall, 4 m. NNE of Wade Bridge. Area 3,530 acres. Pop. 1,154.
ENDENNA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 6 m. N of Bergamo. Pop. 487.
ENDERBY, a parish of Leicestershire, 43 m. SSW of Leicester. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 1,336.
ENDERBY, an island on the NW coast of Australia, in S lat. 20° 35′ 25″, E long. 116° 23′ 5″.
ENDERBY (BAG), a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. NW by N of Spilsby. Area 860 acres. Pop. 102.
ENDERBY (CAPE), a headland on the coast of New Britain, in N lat. 63° 45′, W long. 65° 30′.
ENDERBY (MAVIS), a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. WNW of Spilsby. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 379.
ENDERBY (Wood), a parish in Lincolnshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m, SSE of Horncastle. Area 990 acres. Pop. 233.

ENDERBY'S ISLAND, one of the Auckland group, the second in point of extent, lying to the NE of Auckland island, from which it is separated by a channel 1 m. wide. Its N point is in S lat. 50° 30′. Its length from ENE to WSW is 7 m.; its breadth 31 m. At the back of Sandy-bay, on its S side—which is a 1 m. deep, and has a bottom of hard sand—facing the entrance to Laurie harbour or Port Ross in Auckland, is a large extent of fine grass land, intersected by two streams of water. The grass is 2 or 3 ft. high; and so thick that it is difficult to walk through it. This grass land, and several thousand acres of a similar quality situated at a short distance, will pasture any number of cattle and sheep likely to be sent, and it is satisfactory to know that it is to be had all the year round. About 80 head of horned cattle and 300 sheep have been already landed on this island, and are thriving to an extent that astonished the stockmen who had come in charge of them from Sydney. Snipes are numerous on this island, and crabs abound in Sandy-bay.
ENDERI. See ANDREEWA.
ENDERMO, a port of Japan, on the S coast of the island of Jesso, in N lat. 42° 20'. It forms the NE point of Volcan bay. It was visited by Broughton in 1796.
ENDERSDORF, a village of Moravia, in the circle and 30 m. NW of Troppau.—Also a village of Silesia, in the principality of Neisse, 3 m. S of Ziegenhals. *
ENDERTA, a flat fertile district, in the SE of | Abyssinia, depending on Tigre. Its cap. is Antalo.
ENDFORD, a parish of Wiltshire, 8 m. WNW of Ludgershall. Area 7,880 acres. Pop. 947.
ENDIAN. See HINDIAN.

ENDINGEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, 12 m. NW of Friburg, at an alt. of 620 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,936. Linen is made here.—Also a town of Wurtemberg, 12 m. NE of Rothweil, in the Schwarzwald. Pop. 746.
ENDON, a chapelry in Staffordshire, in the p. and 4½ m. SW of Leek. Pop. 571.
ENDO'R, a village of Syria, S by W of Mount Tabor, under the brow of Little Hermon.
ENDRACHT'S LAND. See EENDRACHT.
ENDRE, a river of France, in the dep. of Var, flowing into the Argens on the l. bank, after a course of 18 m.
ENDRICHOW. See ANDRYCHAU.
ENDRICK, a river in the co. of Stirling, which, after receiving the Blane and other streams, falls into Lochlomond, on the E side, 14 m. from its source.
ENDRINAL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 6 m. S of Salamanca. Pop. 1,200.
ENEGO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 30 m. NNE of Vicenza. Pop. 1,135.
ENERGLYN, a hamlet in the p. of Eglwysilan, Glamorganshire, 2 m. W of Caerphilly. Pop. in 1801, 1,013; in 1841, 808.
ENESEI. See YENESEI.
ENESEISK. See YENESEISK.
ENFADO (SIERRAS-DEL), a chain of mountains in the S part of Old California, which stretch from a point SE of the bay of Magdalena to the Sierrade-Carmelo.
ENFANT-PERDU, an islet off the coast of French Guayana, 9 m. NW of Cayenne.

ENFIELD, a town and parish of Middlesex, 12 m. N of London by the London and Hertford railway. The parish has an area of 12,460 acres. Pop. in 1801, 5,881; in 1841, 9,367. E. chase comprises a large tract of woodland, which in 1779 was disafforested by act of parliament.—Also a v. in the p. of Rathcore, co. Meath, 201 m. W by N of Dublin. Pop. 375.

ENFIELD, a township in Penobscot co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 104 m. NE of Augusta. Pop. 346.—Also a township in Grafton co., in New Hampshire, 42 m. NW of Concord. Pop. 1,514.—Also a township in Hampshire co., in Massachusetts, 75 m. W by S of Boston, on Swift river. Pop. 976.—Also a township in Hartford co., in Connecticut, 18 m. N by E of Hartford. Pop. 2,648.—Also a township in Tompkin's co., in New York, 5 m. W of Ithaca. Pop. 2,340.—Also a v. in Halifax co., N. Carolina, 76 m. NE of Raleigh.—Also a v. in King William co., in Virginia, 24 m. NE of Richmond.
ENFRAS, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 27 m. E of Viseu.
ENFUME' (CAPE), a lofty headland on the E coast of Cape Breton, in N lat. 46° 40'. Bouchette estimates its alt. at 1,500 ft.; Macgregor, at 1,800 ft. Haliburton was told that it exceeded 2,600 ft.

ENGADINE, or INNTHAL, a beautiful valley of Switzerland, in that part of the Grisons called 'the League of God's House,' at an alt. of from 3,500 ft. to 6,100 ft., and extending along, from SW to NE, both sides of the upper valley of the Inn, from the source of that river in the Maloïa, to the gorge of Finstermunz on the confines of Tyrol. It is about 45 m. in length, but in some parts very narrow, not exceeding 1 m. in breadth. The mountains by which it is enclosed on the SE, belong to the Rhætian Alps, those on the NW to the Lepontine Alps; and consist of inaccessible piles of rock, between which are to be seen at intervals immense glaciers. The | zil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 15 m. S of Barba- ENG soil at the foot of the hills, and a little way up their sides, is susceptible of culture, and the pasture lands are rich and nourishing. Forests of oak, beech, birch, and other trees, clothe the upper parts of the mountains; and wood forms one of the principal objects of traffic. It is divided into the two jurisdictions of the Ober E. and Unter E., which are subdivided into 21 parishes. The pop. of the valley in 1842 was 10,596. Inglis has devoted a chapter in his pleasing Travels in Switzerland, &c., to this valley, to which the reader may be referred for details respecting its scenery, and the rural and domestic economy of its inhabitants.
ENGALDI, a village of Abyssmia, 80 m. WNW of Gondar.
ENGANNO (CAPE), the E extremity of the island of Hayti, in N lat. 18° 34′ 30″, W long. 68° 20′ 30″.

ENGANO, one of the Sunda islands to the SW of Sumatra, in S lat. 5° 21'. It is about 30 m. in circumf., and is thickly clothed with forest trees. It is of a triangular form, and moderate height, and is visible at the distance of 15 m. The natives are taller and fairer than the Malays. They dwell in conical houses, resembling bee-hives, supported on posts, and standing singly amid their plantations. They have neat canoes, formed of two planks sewed together, pointed at each end, and provided with outriggers. They have neither cattle, fowls, nor even rice; but appear to subsist entirely on cocoanuts, sugar-cane, sweet potatoes, and fish. An unsuccessful attempt was made, by the English settled in Sumatra, to gain some acquaintance with these islanders, in 1771. A similar attempt had been made by the Dutch in 1645, from Java.—Also an islet in a bay on the N coast of Guinea, in S lat. 2° 33'.
ENGELHARTSZELL, a small town of Upper Austria, in the Hausruck circle, on the Danube, 9 m. E of Passau.
ENGELHAUS, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Elbogen, 2 m. ESE of Carlsbad.

ENGELHOLM, a port of Sweden, in a bay on the E side of the Cattegat, near the mouth of the Rönne-A, 45 m. NW of Christianstad, in N lat. 56° 14' 20". Pop. 1,088.—It has recently been proposed to form a new harbour at the mouth of the Rönne, near to E. The plan of making that river navigable as far as Ringsee, in the middle of Schonen, has also been suggested; and likewise the formation of a railroad from Meuse to Christianstadt and Aahuus, by which the former project of making a canal will fall to the ground, the object of both plans being to establish a communication between the North sea and East sea, and avoid the payment of the Sounddues levied by the Danish government.
ENGELSBERG, a town of Austrian Silesia, at the foot of a mountain of the same name, in the circle and 22 m. WNW of Troppau. Pop. 2,200.
ENGELSTELEN, a small river of the Swiss cant. of Bern, which falls into the Candel near Teutlingen.
ENGELVOR, a small island off the NW coast of Norway.
ENGELWIES, a village of Baden, in the Lake circle, NE of Mösskirch. Pop. 365.

ENGEN, a town of Baden, in the Lake circle, on the Aach. Pop. 1,224. It has some muslin manufactures. It was formerly fortified, and sustained several sieges. The French under Moreau occupied it in October 1796, at the time of that general's celebrated retreat. It was again occupied in March 1799 by the French, under Jourdan; and on the 3d of May, 1800, the Austrians were defeated here by Moreau, when that commander made his last invasion of Germany.
ENGENHO-DO-MATO, a parish and v. of Bra-

ENGISTEIN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 6 m. E of Berne. Pop. 300. There are mineral springs here. St. George's channel, which separates it from Ireland. It is divided from Scotland, on the N, by the Tweed from its mouth to Kerholm; then by Keddonburn, Haddonrig, Blackdown-hill, Merslaw-hill, and Baltonbuss-hill, to the sources of the Kail and the Jed; then by Kershope-burn, Liddalwater, and the Esk, to Todholes; and by the Marchdyke to White Sark on the Solway firth. The general outline of this most important section of the United kingdom is triangular. The base of the triangle is formed by a line, 340 m. in length, drawn from the S. Foreland in Kent, to the Land's-end in Cornwall. The E side may be conceived as formed by a line, 345 m. in length, drawn from Berwick on the NE to the S. Foreland; and the W side by a line of 425 m., drawn from Berwick to the Land's end. From N to S the greatest length of England is 400 m.; in some places the united breadth of England and Wales is 300 m. To the N of the Humber, the breadth is not much more than a third of what has just been specified. Britain, has been given by Dr. Becke: Proportional Acres. part of S Britain. Bedford, Total number of acres in 38,498,572 England and Wales, A little addition would fall to be made to this calculation, from the circumstance of the irregularity or unevenness of the surface of England, and for the mountainous districts of Wales. Dr. Becke supposes, that in the clayey, gravelly, and sandy soils, which predominate on the E side of the kingdom, the increase of surface, from its irregularity or unevenness, does not amount to much more than the 1,000th part; that in the hilly and chalky counties, it scarcely exceeds the 400th part; and that, making a greater allowance for Wales, Westmoreland, and other very mountainous districts, it will not collectively amount to more, at most, than between 120,000 and 150,000 acres. Solway-frith and proceeding S to Braich-y-pwl point, is partly flat, partly bold and elevated. A wide inlet presents itself containing the estuary of the Solway-frith, Morecambe-bay, the estuaries of the Ribble, the Mersey, and the Dee, and, in the very centre of it, the isle of Man. The Ribble was once the most dis- Romans in preference to any other, and emphatically called 'the vulsion of nature. Whittaker, in reference to it observes:ascribing the final ruin of Ribchester to the overwhelming violence of an earthquake. And nothing but such an accident, I most remarkable estuary in the county, and have thrown up that large and broad barrier of sand which crosses the entrance into or 9 m., to the narrow span of 100 yds." Directly S from the isle of Man is the isle of Anglesey, forming one of the cos. of Wales, and separated from the main land by the celebrated Menai straits. Cardigan bay, another immense opening, stretching from Braichy-pwl point to St. David's-head, presents a coast in general rugged, mountainous and wild. Between St. David's-head and the Land's-end another inundation gives space to St. Bride's-bay, Milford-haven, Caermarthen and Swansea bays, the Bristol channel or estuary of the Severn, and Barnstaple bay. The beach along this tract continues mostly high and rocky; off it, are some small and a great portion of the coast of Cornwall and Devonshire has been invaded and swept away by encroachments of the sea. The Scilly islands, it is stated upon good authority, were formerly called the Land's-end, in N lat. 50° 4' 20", W long. 5° 41', the spacious bay of St. Michael opens on the view; and on doubling the with small islands. Opposite to the estuary of Southampton lies the porous, spongy but withal tenacious matter, than the neighbouring sands; and are consequently of such a quality, that when a ship strikes upon them there is little chance of her getting off. A level sandy beach separates the high chalky cliffs which face the coast for a considerable way on each side of the S. Foreland, from those which skirt the bold and rugged shore of the isle of Thanet, —a place formerly detached from the rest of Kent by a navigable river, but now little more than a peninsula. The E point of this isle is the N. Foreland, between which and Orfordness stretches a large gulf into which the rivers Medway, Thames, Crouch, and Maden, discharge their waters; and along which the shore, after passing the isle of Sheppey, is for the most part flat and marshy. From Orfordness to the spacious inlet called the Wash, the coast assumes a circular direction, and presents an intermixture of low ground, sandy hillocks, and clayey precipices. Crossing the Wash, and keeping the level beach of Lincolnshire, we come to the Humber, beyond which, with the exception of Spurn-head, Flamboroughhead, and Scarborough, there are few indentations or promontories of note. Scarborough stands on a vast rock, projecting into the sea; but Flamborough-head is a far more magnificent object, being formed of limestone of a snowy whiteness and stupendous height, visible far off at sea. From Spurn-head to Flamboroughhead the coast is flat; from this to Bamborough-castle it is first bold and precipitous, and then descends to low cliffs of sandstone and other materials; and from this to Berwick-upon-Tweed it is tame and sandy. Not far from the shore, opposite to the Northumbrian coast, appear the Coquet-isle, the Fern-isles, and Holyisle. The inroads of the sea on the E coast of England have been quite as remarkable as in any other quarter of the island. "The abbey of Whitby, at its first erection in 958, is reported to have been 1 m. from the sea; but the distance from the verge of Whitby E cliff to the nearest part of the abbey, measured in the line of transept, was found in 1816 to be little more than 200 yds. Along the coast-line of Yorkshire, from Bridlington-quay to Spurn-point, the shore has no important inlet or projection, and consists of beds of clay, gravel, sand, and chalk-rubble; exposed to a strong current from the N, as well as to the uncontrolled action of the waves, the annual devastation committed here is very extensive. Of the vs. of Auburn, Hartburn, and Hyde, in the bay of Bridlington, only the remembrance remains. Several places on the shore preserve, in the termination of their names, a memorial of meres or fresh-water lakes once having existed in their neighbourhood,—as Skipsea, Kilnsea, and Withernsea,—the Scandinavian sjo signifying 'a lake,'-but the sea has broken into these meres, and absorbed them, though recesses on the shore seem to mark the spots they once occupied. The mere at Hornsea still survives; but this place, which was once several miles inland, has been brought within half-a-mile of the water's edge, and the hamlet of Hornsea-beck been utterly destroyed. The waste of coast amounts to about 4 yds. a-year; and farmers may be met with who have witnessed the corn wave where the sea now prevails. The depredations of the ocean towards Spurn-point, at the entrance of the Humber estuary, have been still more considerable; nor is it unlikely that the Point will ere long become an island. Ravenspur, an important place in this locality, has long since been lost, with a number of other places in the vicinity, belonging to Bristol priory, and the site of the priory itself has been totally swept away." Pennant remarks: "The site, and even the very names of several places, once towns of note upon the Humber, are now only recorded in history. Ravenspur was at one time a rival to Hull, and a port so very considerable, in 1332, that Edward Baliol and the confederated English barons sailed from hence to invade Scotland; and Henry IV., in 1399, made choice of this port to land at, to effect the deposal of Richard II.; yet the whole has long since been devoured by the merciless ocean. Extensive sands, dry at low water, are to be seen in their stead." Mr. Lyell makes a remarkable statement respecting Sheringham, on the coast of Norfolk: "I ascertained in 1829 some facts which throw light upon the rate at which the sea gains on the land. It was computed, when the present inn was built in 1805, that it would require 70 years for the sea to reach the spot, the mean loss of land being calculated, from previous observations, to be somewhat less than 1 yd. annually. The distance between the house and the sea was 50 yards; but no allowance was made for the slope of the ground being from the sea, in consequence of which the waste was naturally accelerated every year as the cliff grew lower, there being at every succeeding period less matter to remove when portions of equal area fell down. Between the years 1824 and 1829, no less than 17 yds. were swept away, and only a small garden was then left between the building and the sea. There is now a depth of 20 ft.—sufficient to float a frigate— at one point in the harbour of the port, where only 48 years ago there stood a cliff 50 ft. high, with houses upon it." The advantages derived by Britain from its insular position, and the conformation of its coasts, have been well set forth by an intelligent German. "It is certain," says Dr. Carus, "that Europe never would have become the centre of human civilization had it not been for the peculiarity of its figure and situation, so remarkably surrounded by seas, and stretching almost like the outline of a human form between the northern and southern waters. There are elements enough of a similar description, in its outlines by land and sea, which again abundantly prove in how far England, of all European states, is by far the bestadapted to attain the greatest possible development in naval power and in the arts of navigation. One of the most important elements of this progress consists in the number and variety of those bays and arms of the sea which like deep rivers penetrate far into the interior of the country. It is only when one has made the circuit of the English coasts, by land or sea, and has had daily opportunities of observing what sharp and decisive limits are drawn between sea and land, and how few opportunities are offered for such free transition from one to the other as might be supposed would exist from their absolute contact,—it is only when one has seen that no ship can come to land, and sometimes not even a boat touch the coast without great danger, that any idea can be formed of the vast importance and immense value of those bays and inlets which constitute as it were the connecting link, and facilitate reciprocal communication. The coasts are often inaccessible in consequence of dangerous sand-banks; the restless surge, at other places, beating on the rocky shore under the influence of the smallest breeze, prevents the possibility of passing either from land to sea or from sea to land; whilst in other places, again, steep or precipitous rocks, or a strand strewed with pieces of rock, make all approach impossible. Within these bays the raging waves become gradually calm; by means of them even the largest ships are able to ascend so far into the country that the productions of the remotest quarters of the world are conveyed into the very heart of the national industry, and the manufactures of the looms and forges of Great Britain are received and carried to the extremities of the earth; on their banks it is that sites are chosen for the foundation of great and flourishing cities, and the most admirable situations afforded for the building and repair of ships. Let us lay before us the map of England and Scotland, and reckon the multitude of bays which, like vast rivers of salt water, stretch far into the land,-these inlets, sometimes short and sometimes long-known by the names of rivers, friths, or mouths-which indent the country; let us also have the opportunity of personal inspection, and observe how gradually their sea nature passes over, and changes into that of the interior; and much will be gained towards an understanding of the original destination and calling of England to be a country of naval power and mistress of the seas. It will then be seen how often the wild and stormy sea which beats against precipitous rocks, as at Dartmouth, becomes at last as still as a pond, and terminates among rich meadows and woody hills; or how that which rushes on between dangerous sand-banks, further on its course, becomes a deep and safe harbour, and laves the docks of immense trading cities, as at Liverpool and London; and the conviction will always become stronger, that it is only a people to whom nature has offered so many facilities for intercourse between sea and land, that can have obtained the call to struggle with all their might and skill to obtain and secure naval pre-eminence. I must further add, as a particular element in the formation of these bays, that they only receive the waters of very small rivers, and often nothing more than large brooks, and that they are therefore far more permanent in their form, and better calculated for havens and harbours of refuge for ships; as small streams are incapable of choking-up, or even sensibly lessening the depth of such bays by any quantity of sand which they can convey: whilst, in the case of great rivers, the processes of accumulation and deposit, and the formation of deltas at their mouths, are continually going forward. Having thus obtained an important element for the proper understanding of England, and the English people in particular, I must further remark, that these coasts are better fitted than most others to afford the most complete view of the great phenomena of the ocean in general. The perpetual motion of the sea,—the rhythmical beat of its waves,—the vast power of its surge,—and the wondrous relations of its ebb and flow,—are things which have here first become thoroughly intelligible to me: and I reckon all this as a real and substantial contribution to the means of comprehending the life of the earth in general." General features.] The general aspect of England is varied and delightful. In some parts verdant plains extend as far as the eye can reach, watered by copious streams, and covered by innumerable cattle. In others, the pleasing vicissitudes of gently rising hills and bending vales, fertile in corn, waving with wood, and interspersed with meadows, offer delightful landscapes of opulence and beauty. Some tracts abound with prospects of the more romantic kind,— lofty mountains, craggy rocks, deep narrow dells, and tumbling torrents; nor are there wanting, as a contrast, the gloomy features of black moors, and wide uncultivated heaths. — The general aspect of Wales, as distinguished from England, is bold, romantic, and mountainous. It consists of continued ranges of lofty mountains, and impending crags, intersected by numerous and deep ravines and extensive valleys. These ranges generally extend in a direction from SE to NW, having their most abrupt declivity facing the latter quarter. Numerous projecting ridges expand laterally, on various points of the compass. Mountains.] Through the whole length of England and Wales, there are groups of mountains and hills, which, when viewed on a grand scale, may be considered as forming one chain extending along the W side of the kingdom from Cornwall to Cumberland. In this chain all the highest mountains of England and Wales are situated. The breadth of the kingdom may also be considered as determined by two lower ranges of hills, one of which extends from Dorsetshire into Kent, while the other stretches, in a waving line, from the island of Portland to the Wolds in the E. R. of Yorkshire. The line which is formed by this latter chain of hills passes on the W side of Wiltshire and Oxfordshire, and through Northamptonshire, Leicestershire, Nottinghamshire, nearly to Scarborough. The Bristol channel, and the low grounds of Lancashire and Cheshire, break and divide the great western chain of mountains into # three parts, which have been denominated the Northern, the Cambrian, and the Devonian range. Northern range.] The Northern range enters Cumberland from Scotland, and passing through that co. and Westmoreland, extends its branches into Northumberland and Durham. The branch which enters Northumberland composes the Cheviot hills. It is not easy to form an estimate of the extent of these hills, as they unite with the moorland district of Northumberland to the S, and are continued to the W by similar green hills in Scotland. On the supposition that their bases occupy a circle of 15 m. in diam., their area will be from 150 to 200 sq. m. In form, many of them are conical, some of them nearly perfect cones, while the shape of others is very irregular; in general, however, they are pointed, their sides are steep and smooth, and their bases nearly in contact one with another. The soil on these mountains, except at the summits, where points of rock and loose stones appear, is fertile; from base to summit they present a refreshing and rich green sward.—That chain of northern mountains which may properly be called the Cumberland chain, commences at Geltsdale-forest, 14 m. SE of Carlisle, and passes on the W of Durham and Yorkshire. The surface of these mountains in general is excessively rugged, and in their disposition there is nothing of a lengthened ridge or continuous chain. The appearance of the whole is that of a congeries of broken and mostly pointed masses, united at their bases, except in those places where they are divided by the lakes which are scattered among them. The Cumbrian hills resemble the Cheviot in the green sward with which they are covered; but Skiddaw, like Cheviot, is partially clothed with heath. The extent of this tract of mountains in Cumberland, Westmoreland, and Lancashire, is about 500 or 600 sq. m .- The northern range, taking it in its utmost extent, forms by its mountains and valleys the fascinating scenery round the lakes of Cumberland and Westmoreland, the gloomy grandeur of Craven in Yorkshire, and the romantic dales of Derbyshire. The general height of these mountains is from 3,000 to 3,400 ft. According to the barometrical admeasurements of Mr. Dalton, the heights of the most remarkable are as follows: Sea-fell, Cumberland, 3,240 ft. and the second 3,225 Helvellyn, do., . These admeasurements differ somewhat from those given in the Trigonometrical Survey.—The S division of the Northern range is divided from the N division by the valley of the Ribble, the Craven, and the valley of the Aire: its W boundary is formed by the lower, or vale lands of Lancashire and Cheshire; its E, by the manufacturing districts of Yorkshire; and its S, by the upper grounds of Derbyshire and Staffordshire. The extent of these hills is above 60 m.; their width is extremely irregular, being upwards of 20 m. from the feet of the Cheshire hills, to the hills near Sheffield, whereas between Blackstoneedge and Huddersfield they merely form a ridge. The highest hills in Derbyshire are Kinderscout near Hayfield, Axe-Edge near Buxton, and Whinhill and Mam-Tor near Castleton; but they are much lower than the mountains of Cumberland, the highest part of Derbyshire not being more than 2,100 ft. above the sea. As we approach the Alpine districts on the W of England, we meet with some hills which it may be III.

ENGLAND AND WALES. proper to notice before we proceed to the consideration of the Cambrian range. The Malvern-hills, which are situated partly in Gloucestershire, but principally in Herefordshire and Worcestershire, are from 1,100 to 1,300 ft. high. The highest are those distinguished by the names of Herefordshire and Worcestershire beacons; the former rising nearly 1,260 ft., and the latter 1,300 ft. above the level of the plain. The Malvern-hills extend for 10 m., rising on the E side from the flat country which forms the vale of the Severn. They are connected on the W side with a range of lower hills which extend several miles into Herefordshire.—The Cotswold and the Stroudwater hills, in Gloucestershire, are by some regarded as a continuation of the central chain, proceeding S from Derbyshire, and passing through Gloucestershire into Wiltshire, there swelling into the Salisbury downs, and afterwards running W towards the Land's-end in Cornwall. Viewed in this light, they may be regarded as connecting the Northern with the Devonian range. The extent of the Cotswold-hills, from Broadway-hill to near Tetbury, is 30 m. Their area is about 200,000 acres; their surface is billowy, and their climate, if the natural elevation of the land be considered, unusually mild. Their sides abound with springs, "almost every dip has its rill, and every valley its brook." The Stroudwater-hills partake in some parts of the character and features of the Cotswold-hills, and in other parts they partake of the vale character.—The Wrekin in Shropshire is so much higher than the surrounding hills that it appears to rise alone from the middle of the plain. It is craggy at the top. Its form is that of a long oval, pointing nearly N and S. The most precipitous side of this mountain is the E; its height is reckoned about 1,200 ft. The Wrekin may be considered as the N extremity of a ridge lying in the same line with it, and consisting of the hills of Frodsley, Caradoc, &c. Each of these has the long diameter from N to S. They are craggy at top, and ascend from the plain of Salop abruptly, at an angle of about 60°. Cambrian range.] The Cambrian range of mountains is considerably more elevated in N. than in S. Wales, extending through Caernaryonshire and Merionethshire, and declining in height as they pass through Cardiganshire. The direction of this range in Caernarvonshire is NE, from Bardsey-island to a promontory in the bay of Conway. The mountains composing it are the highest of any in Wales, and gradually ascend from each extremity of the range towards the centre, which is formed and occupied by Snowdon, the loftiest of all. The alt. of the highest point of this celebrated mountain is 3,571 ft. from the high-water mark on Caernarvon quay. It is composed of various cliffs, rising one above another; and that particular peak, to which the name of Snowdon is given, little exceeds in height several of the summits that surround it on all sides.—From Snowdon, a line of mountains extends by the sea to Plinlimmon, a boundary of N. Wales; of these mountains, the most lofty and the most celebrated is Cader-Idris. In height [2,914 ft.] it is the second in Wales. It rises on the sea-shore about 1 m. above Torvyn. Its ascent is gradual, first in a N direction for about 3 m., then for about 10 m. ENE. From its summit, a branch, nearly 3 m. long, spreads out in a SW direction, parallel to the main ridge. On all sides it is steep and craggy, but the S side is almost perpendicular. On the E of N. Wales, the hills do not attain nearly such an elevation, and gradually decline to the hills of Shropshire, which have been already noticed.—In S. Wales, a chain proceeds to near Cardiff. Its direction is nearly S, a small branch diverging to the W. This chain is of far inferior elevation. On the E of S. Wales are the hills of Herefordshire. Devonian range.] The Devonian range of mountains passes through part of Somersetshire, and through Devonshire and Cornwall, and terminates at the Land's-end. The highest part of this range is formed by the mountains of Dartmoor in Devonshire, the altitudes of the principal eminences being from 1,500 to 1,800 ft. On approaching this tract from the S and SE, the eye is bewildered by an extensive vale, exhibiting gigantic tors, large surfaces covered with vast masses of scattered granite, and the W district, and include between 300,000 and 400,000 acres of uncultivated land. In the highest parts to the N and W are vast tracts of wet swampy ground, which supply the inhabitants with peat for fuel. From Dartmoor the chain extends to the extremity of Cornwall. The only other hills in this range are the Mendip-hills, situated in Somersetshire, stretching from Selwood on the E to Axbridge on the W, and from Redminster on the N to Glastonbury on the S. Cross ranges.] It has already been observed that the breadth of the island may be supposed to be determined, in the S and E parts, by two ranges of hills, considerably lower than that range which determines its length: according to some, one of these chains extends from Dorsetshire to Kent, while the other is represented as stretching from the isle of Portland to the Wolds; but according to others, the three loftiest and most naked ridges of the SE quarter of the island commence on Salisbury plain,—an amazing extent of high and chalky land stretching 25 m. E to Winchester, and 28 m. W to Weymouth; its breadth, in some places, being from 35 to 40 m. The first of the ridges that takes its rise from this quarter, after intersecting Hampshire and Sussex, terminates at Beachy-head; that part of it which lies in Sussex forming the famous South Downs, which are nearly 50 m. long, and about 5 or 6 m. broad. The second ridge extends to the E shore of Kent, and forms the Surrey-hills or downs, little less celebrated for the goodness of their sheep-pasture than the South Downs. A little to the E of Farnham, in this co., they are merely a narrow ridge called the Hog's-back; but as they penetrate more deeply into it, their breadth becomes considerable. Their S side, in general, is steep; their N, gently sloping. The third range, which by some is supposed to take its rise on the Wiltshire-downs, crosses Oxfordshire into Norfolk. The hills of Gogmagog in Cambridgeshire belong to this range. The Chiltern-hills, another upland tract of considerable elevation, extend from Tring in Hertfordshire to Henley in Oxfordshire. Moorlands.] The most extensive and celebrated moorlands are those of Northumberland; the moorlands of the five counties, as Mr. Marshall denominates them; the moorlands of Lancashire; the E and W moorlands of Yorkshire; and the moorlands of Staffordshire. The moorlands of Northumberland may be divided into the W and the S; the latter, however, unite with, and therefore properly belong to the moorlands of the five counties, according to Mr. Marshall's arrangement. The W moorlands occupy more than one-third of the surface of the co. The N. Tyne rises in their NW quarter, and runs nearly through the middle of them .- The moorlands of the five counties-viz. Durham, Northumberland, Cumberland, Westmoreland, and the N part of Yorkshire-form one united mass of mountain-heights, separated on the N from the main body of the Northumberland moorlands by the valley of the Irthing, and on the S, by the valley of the Greta, from the W moorlands of Yorkshire. The moorlands of Lancashire are separated into two divisions, by the valley of the Ribble. The N division is insulated, being separated from the W moorlands of Yorkshire by Craven, and the district which intervenes between Craven and Lonsdale. This district forms their boundary on the N; on the W, they are bounded by the cultivated lands of Lancashire. The S division of the moorlands unites itself with the S range of the mountains of the N of England; already described. The E moorlands of Yorkshire are bounded, on the W, by the vale of York; on the E, by the seacoast district of the N riding of this co.; on the N by the vale of Stockton; and on the S, by the limestone-lands of E. Yorkshire. Their extent, if the valleys which lie among them are taken into the calculation, may be about 400 or 500 sq. m. The elevation of this minor class of English mountains is much below that of the Cumberland mountains. Their surface is tame, their soil poor, and in natural economy they resemble the moorlands of Northumberland and Durham, rather than those of the W. R. of Yorkshire.—The W moorlands of Yorkshire, which form a tract of black, heathy mountains, are bounded on the N by the valley of the Greta; on the S, they are divided from the more S mountains of the N of England by the valley of the Aire; on the NW they extend into Westmoreland and Cumberland; on the W and SW their boundary is formed by the district of Craven, which contains the mountains of Ingleborough and Whernside, forming part of the great Northern chain on the E. These moorlands shelve down to the cultivated lands that constitute the W bank of the vale of York. The elevation of the W boundaries of these mountains is very considerable; inferior, indeed, to the elevation of the Cumberland mountains, but much greater than the elevation of the E moorlands of Yorkshire. Their surface, however, is tame; but from this character must be excepted their W margin, which is much broken and strongly featured.—The moorlands of Staffordshire lie on the N part of that co., to the N of a line drawn from Uttoxeter to Newcastle under Lyne. Their elevation must be considerable, as some of the numerous streams which take their rise in this tract of country run into opposite seas. That part of them which lies between Cheadle and Oak-moor, consists of an immense number of rude heaps of gravel thrown together without order or form into sudden swells and deep glens. To the N of Oak-moor the calcareous part of the moorlands begins, reaching in length from the Weaverhills to Longner, and in breadth from Dove to Morridge, and including 50 or 60 sq. m. mentioned as being considered by some to be the N extremity of the chalk-hills of England; indeed, their natural character is differs from that of their W extreme. Here they end abruptly, in tall, steep cliffs, the mass having the appearance of having and scattered at several miles' distance from the body of the hills. The outlines of the wolds are irregular. If measured from the vale-lands of Holderness to those of the vale of York, and from those of the vale of Pickering to the calcareous lands at their S extremity, their area cannot be less than 500 sq. m. In elevation, surface, soil, and substrata, the wolds are very similar to the chalk-hills of Kent, Surrey, Sussex, Hampshire, Wiltshire, and Dorsetshire. Rivers. The most considerable rivers of England are the Thames, Severn, Medway, Trent, Ouse, Tyne, Tees, Wear, Mersey, Dee, Avon, Eden, and Derwent, which, aided by an extensive system of canalnavigation, afford easy access into the interior of the country, and enable the most inland districts to communicate readily with the sea as well as with one another. In the last edition of Camden's Britannia that was published by himself (in 1605) there is a table, from which it appears that there are upwards of 550 rivers and rivulets in England and Wales. The reader is referred to our separate articles on the leading rivers of England and Wales. Lakes.] There are few isolated pieces of fresh water deserving the name of lakes in England. Westmoreland and Cumberland, however, are celebrated for their lakes, which, during the summer, attract numerous visitors. Winandermere, the most extensive piece of fresh water in England, is 101 m. in a straight line down its middle, and from 1 to 2 m. in breadth. Ulswater is about 9 m. in length, and varies in breadth from a 1/4 m. to 2 m. The smaller lakes are Derwentwater, Bassenthwaitewater, Overwater, Loweswater, Crummuckwater, Buttermere, Ennerdalewater, Wastwater, and Devockwater. Whittlesea lake, in Huntingdonshire, is a shallow piece of water covering an immense tract of country. Climate.] With respect to climate, England, from its situation in the N part of the temperate zone, cannot enjoy long or in great vividness the genial influence of the sun; while, being an island, it is exposed to great variations of dryness and moisture, as well as of heat and cold, at all seasons; indeed, its atmosphere is inclined to be chilly and damp, and is therefore not so favourable to the ripening as to the growth of the productions of the earth. There is, perhaps, however, no country in Europe which displays such a rich and beautiful verdure for so large a portion of the year; since the rigour of its winter is seldom so great or so lasting as to destroy this verdure by cold, and the heats of its summer, interrupted by cooler weather, and refreshed by frequent showers, do not wither up the grass. On the continent of Europe, the period when the different seasons may be expected to arrive, the mode in which they will respectively commence, and their durations uninterrupted by unseasonable weather, can safely be predicted and relied upon; but in England it is otherwise. The winter-months, indeed, are generally reckoned to be December, January, and February; the spring-months, March, April, and May; the summer-months, June, July, and August; and the autumn-months, September, October, and November; but it never happens that the seasons respectively confine themselves to these months. Not unfrequently, in the month of January or February, a foreigner, unaccustomed to our climate, would imagine that spring was about to commence; the air is mild and balmy, -the buds begin to burst forth,-the birds, which the rigour of winter had kept from sight, again make their appearance,—and the whole face of nature seems to rejoice: this, perhaps, continues for the space of a week or two, when suddenly winter resumes its power. Open and mild weather is also not unfrequently seen even in December, especially towards the beginning of that month. In one respect, however, our winters differ, even when most severe, from the winters of countries under the same latitude on the continent; for while the sea-ports of Holland and Germany are every winter locked up with ice, those of England are never known to suffer this inconvenience. In fact, we lie between the same parallels of lat. as the ice-bound coast and winter-stricken soil of Labrador, yet enjoy on our SW shores almost a perpetual spring. It is to be expected that in the N of England the spring, summer, and autumn will be more backward, less certain, and of shorter duration, than in the S parts of the kingdom. The temperature of the N is not only colder, but it is also moister; the winters are likewise more severe, and of longer duration; so that, to the N of the Mersey and the Humber, the seasons of spring and summer may be reckoned to be nearly a fortnight later in their commencement than they are in the S and SW counties; while the autumn is shorter, and more frequently interrupted with frosts during the night. It may be doubted, however, whether the winter in the N of England is more severe, when compared with the S of England, in the same proportion as the spring and summer are longer and warmer; at least, in the S counties, particularly in those which lie in the vicinity of London, the winters are nearly equal in severity, though perhaps not in length, to the winters of the cos. to the N of the Mersey and the Humber. Besides the difference between the climate of the N and S of England, there is also a difference between that of the E and W cos. The climate of the latter is more moist, and at the same time milder, than the climate of the E cos. This circumstance arises from three causes: the vicinity of the W cos. to the great Atlantic ocean,-the extreme prevalence of W winds, -and the ridge of hills, already described, which runs along the W side of the kingdom. The Atlantic saturates the W winds with immense quantities of vapours, which, intercepted in their passage by the range of hills, fall in rain. The E side of the kingdom, not receiving these winds, till they have given out their vapours, is much less subject to rain; but, on the other hand, from being more exposed to the cold and piercing E winds, its temperature is less pleasant, especially during the summer months. It is well known that the temp. of the ocean is more equable than that of the land; and, of course, at least in our latitudes, milder during winter, and not so warm during the summer: hence, the W cos., being exposed to the winds from the Atlantic ocean, generally experience a winter rather moist than severe; and snow seldom lies in this part of the kingdom for any length of time. This remark applies with peculiar force to the SW cos. of England, particularly Cornwall and Devonshire, which being exposed to the winds both from the Bristol and English channel, is favoured with uncommonly mild winters. Mr. Whitley, however, states that the mean annual temp. of the SW coast of England, at the level of the sea, is about 52°, and, under nearly similar circumstances at the Orkney islands, from carefully recorded observations, it is found to be 46° 3'; thus there is a decrease of annual temp., amounting to 5° 7', from the S to the N extremity of Great Britain, extending through 9° of lat., or 635 m., which gives a decrease of 1° for every 111 m. The mean temp, increases from the E to the W coasts. At Greenwich it is 49°, at Penzance 51° 8', at Cork 54° 4', being at the rate of 1° increase for every 66 m. Thus the mean temp. of the British islands increases from E to W, twice as much in the same distance as it does from N to S, other things being equal. Fall of rain.] It has been already observed, that in the W cos. much more rain falls in the course of the year than in the E: it may be added, that a greater proportion of rain falls on the NW coast, as compared with the SE side of the island. The proportion of rain which fell in the course of a year at Townley in Lancashire was measured, upwards of a century ago, and compared with the quantity which fell during the same space of time at Axminster in Essex. on an average of 6 years, from 1700 to 1705 inclusive, it appeared that there fell at Townley 421 in., while at Axminster there fell only 19 in. The mean quantity of rain that has been observed to fall in the co. of Rutland, in the course of the year, is 201 in. At Selbourne, in Hampshire, where the country is rather hilly, the average quantity of rain which fell annually, between 1780 and 1786, was 36.98 in. In Staffordshire, the annual fall of rain generally exceeds 36 in.; whereas, in Worcestershire, under the same meridian of longitude, it is short of 30 in., -a proof of what was remarked above, that those N cos. which lie as far to the W as the S cos., in general have a moister climate. The midland cos. in England receive, as might be expected, nearly the medium quantity of rain that falls on the W and E coasts; or rather, perhaps, the quantity that falls in them approaches nearer to the quantity that falls on the E than the W coast. Even in Derbyshire, though a mountainous district, much less rain falls than in Lancashire, or even Staffordshire or Shropshire. The cos. in the vicinity of the metropolis are subject to comparatively trifling falls of rain. Perhaps the co. in which the least quantity of rain falls is Norfolk. There are certainly not sufficient data on which to ground any statement of the annual average quantity of rain that falls in England at large. Dr. Halley supposes it to be 22 in.; but this is evidently greatly below the average. Mr. Dalton, with much greater probability, fixes it at 31.3 in. Mr. Whitley sums up the evidence collected by him on this subject thus: "On the cultivated soil of the SW of England about 44 in. of rain fall annually. On the great E plain of England,-having Greenwich and Chiswick on the S, and Boston on the N, at which places careful observations have been made, -24 in. are the yearly quantity. Along the fertile lowlands of the E coast of England and Scotland, as far as Inverness, with some minor variations, the same amount is received by the soil. On the W coast, the grauwacke hills of the S of Scotland, Cumberland, Wales, and Cornwall, attract and condense the clouds passing from the Atlantic, and so receive the largest quantity. Generally the western hills and the adjoining lowlands have more open soils than the midland districts, and require more moisture. There is an old adage, that 'Cornwall will take a shower every day of the week, and two on the Sunday.' These hills also form a sort of natural drainage, drawing off from the clouds that excess of water which would be injurious to the stiff wheat soils of the midland cos. The general elevation of the rain-clouds appears to be from 500 to 2,000 ft. The detached mountain-bosses of Ireland and the lowlands near are the wettest parts of that country. The central plain not rising higher than 200 ft., permits these clouds to pass onward with a casual, yet frequent tribute, and it is not till they are intercepted by the Cumberland mountains that the abundance of their contents is discharged in torrents of rain. The hills of the Pennine chain shelter and protect the vale of York from these libations of nature. The distribution of the annual quantity of rain through the different months has an important influence on seed-time, the ripening and harvesting of corn, &c. The months of spring form the driest season of the year. In the midland and E dis- PROPERTY OF THE PROPERTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF tricts, March is a dry month; in the SW cos., April has the least amount of rain; in the lake-district of Cumberland, May and February are the driest months. The month of July has nearly its full share,—a twelfth part of the whole; and August is still wetter; so that during the ripening of corn, and at harvest, a larger quantity of rain falls than is desirable for the perfection of the grain-crops, but not more than is requisite for the roots, which in these months require moisture for a rapid growth. November stands prominently out as a very wet month in Devon and Cornwall; but it has not this extreme character further E, and does not in Ireland show any excess of moisture over December. The autumnal rain sets in earlier around the Cumberland hills, and, judging from Swansea, on the Welsh mountains also. In these districts October is the wettest month. The general result of the observations which have been made is, that there is a gradual change in the distribution of rain amongst the various seasons from W to E. On the SW of Ireland and at Cork, the largest amount is deposited in the winter-months; on the W coast of England and Scotland autumnal rain prevails; on the E parts of England the summer-months receive the greatest proportion of the annual quantity. The effect produced by the elevation of the land on the fall of rain may be thus generally stated:-From the sea at Whitehaven to the wettest part of the Cumberland lake district, the annual quantity increases from 47 to 146 inches! From the lands at an alt. of about 300 ft. in Cornwall, to about 1,500 ft. on the Dartmoor-hills, the yearly amount rises from 44 to 70 in. At Kinfauns-castle, Scotland, 140 ft. high, 25 in. fall annually; on an adjoining hill, 600 ft. above the sea, the quantity deposited is 41 in. The annual quantity of rain is, however, almost as widely distributed through the year on the E as on the W coast." The nature of the climate of England with regard to moisture, cannot, however, be accurately determined, unless the quantity of dew that falls annually be also ascertained. On this point there are few observations or experiments of sufficient accuracy and duration to be relied upon. Dr. Hales thinks, that the quantity of dew that falls on moist earth in this country is 3.28 in.; but Mr. Dalton justly remarks, that this quantity is too small, as the dew which is deposited on grass is much more copious than what falls on moist earth. Hence, he is disposed to take the dew at 5 in. annually, or 22,000,000 tons; and thus to reckon that 36 in. of water is deposited, at a medium, annually on the surface of the earth in England and Wales, reckoning 31 for rain, and 5 for dew, which is equal to 28 cubic m., or 115,000 millions of tons in weight nearly. As it is evident that all the water which falls in the form of rain or dew, must either be carried into the sea by some river, or be raised in vapour, Mr. Dalton has endeavoured to ascertain what proportion is disposed of by the former means, in order to determine the quantity of evaporation. According to his calculations, the Thames carries annually into the ocean, 166,624,128,000 cubic ft. of water, or little more than 1-25th part of all the rain and dew in England and Wales in a year. Mr. D. likewise estimates that the water carried off by all the rivers in England and Wales may amount to nine times that carried off by the Thames; or 13 in. of rain. Hence it follows, that the annual evaporation in this country is equal to 23 inches of water. Temperature.] With respect to the temp. of the atmosphere, as ascertained by the thermometer, it may be remarked in general, that over the whole of the country, the heat of the months of July and August is greater than the heat of any other month; and that a greater degree of cold commonly prevails towards the end of December and the beginning of January, than at any other period of the year. Perhaps the district in which the therm. rises to the greatest height during the summer, is that in the immediate vicinity of the metropolis, rather, however, to the W than to the E of it. With respect to cold, during the winter, the NE cos. are more liable to great degrees of it than the NW; and it may generally be remarked, that there is not nearly so great a difference between the N and S of England, in the temp. of the winter, as in the temp. of the summer months. Mr. Whitley remarks, that "the annual amount of heat on the coast-line of Great Britain is remarkably equal. At Penzance the mean temp. is 51° 8'; Gosport, 51° 8'; Boston, 49° 6'; Leith, 48° 3'; Aberdeen, 49° 1'; Glasgow, 49° 2'; Whitehaven, 49° 1'; Isle of Man, 49° 8'; Dublin, 49° 1'; and at Swansea, 51°. Thus on a coast-line of about 2,000 m. the variation is only 4°. In passing from the coast inland a considerable diminution of temp. often takes place. Leaving Aberdeen at 49° 1', a temp of 47° 3' is found at Clunie Manse, and 45° at Alford, and that within a distance of 25 m. In the middle of England the mean of the year is also from 2° to 4° colder than places situated on the coast. This arises partly from the effect of elevation, and from the modifying influences of sea breezes which the coast lands enjoy. The annual mean temp. of a country is, however," Mr. W. continues, "but a slender criterion from which to form an estimate of its climate, and is especially defective when the influence of climate on vegetation is considered. Penzance and Vienna have the same mean temp.; but the country around Vienna-the upper Hungarian plain-has a summer temperature 10° above Penzance. In the excessively cold winter of 1796, when the Thames was frozen, the temp. of the year in this country fell short of the average by only 1°. The increase of winter cold in various parts of these islands-and which is often so severe as to convert a productive soil in one position into a state of comparative barrenness in another-cannot mainly be attributed to a difference of latitude, for where all things are equal, as on the sea coast, a great equality of temperature prevails. It, therefore, becomes obvious that the severe winter temp. of various districts arises from other causes, amongst which the most prominent is the effect of elevation. The observations [made by Mr. Whitley] tend to fix the height of the snow-line on the S of England at 5,400 ft.; and in the N of Scotland at about 4,000 ft. In the month of January the snow-line descends in S. Wales, Devon, and Cornwall to 2,700 ft. above the sea; in Scotland to 1,620 ft; and on the E coast of England to 1,080 ft. It is obvious, therefore, that the high lands of Wales, the N of England and Scotland, must be exposed to a severe and rigorous winter—the fluctuating character of which renders it still more detrimental to vegetable and animal existence. The summer temp, of the cultivated lands of Great Britain varies from 64° to 54°; on mountainous districts it is still lower. These limits include a climate well-adapted to bring wheat to full perfection, and also a condition of heat where only oats and inferior kinds of barley can be judiciously raised. Much of our land, particularly in Scotland, is thus on the very verge of the profitable cultivation of wheat. From the S of England to the Moray frith the summer temp. decreases on the coast line at the level of the sea, about 5°, being a decrease of 1° for every 100 m. Extending our survey inland, it appears that the E cos. and the midland cos. around Bedfordshire, have, as a whole, the highest summer-temp. in England. But it is in the English vales where the most genial summer heat abounds. These, from their low, sheltered position, and from the geological structure of the soil, are the most highly favoured parts of Britain's favoured isle. In the warmest region of these islands, and where the highest winter temp. exists—on the SW of Ireland-12 or more species of plants occur in a wild state which are natives of the N of Spain. On the SW of England, and SE of Ireland a vegetation exists intimately related to that of the coasts of Normandy and Brittany, and not found elsewhere in the British isles. On the elevated hills of Wales, Scotland, and some of the highest points of Ireland, the plants entirely differ from those of the low lands, and are analogous to the vegetation of Scandinavia and the Alps. In the high regions of Scotland the grouse, the ptarmigan, and the Alpine hare are found in the several zones of temperature for which nature has fitted them. The Germanic type forms the predominating vegetation of the kingdom, occurring alone or in common with groups of other plants. On the SW coast of England greenhouse-plants flourish with less care than in other places, and myrtles, geraniums, and other tender exotics are constantly exposed during the winter, and flower well the following summer. A considerable number of plants from New Holland and New Zealand have flourished in the open air for many years at Penzance." Dr. Thomas Young states, that the mean temperature of the six winter-months, from October to March, at London, is 43° 5'; while at Dawlish, on the S coast of Devonshire, it is 45° 3'; and at Ilfracombe, on the Bristol channel, it is so high as 59°. From November to March, the mean temp. of London is 42°6; of Penzance, in Cornwall, 48°1; from January to March, it is at London 37°9; at Penzance, 48°5; and at Sidmouth, in Devonshire, 41°7;—during February and March, it is at London 41°5; and at Clifton, 42°5; -from October to December, at London, 47°; and at Sidmouth, 45°7;—from December to February, the mean temp. at London is 39°-7. In the most sheltered parts of Devonshire, during winter, the temp, is 1°5 above that of London; in the coldest months, at Penzance, it is 4°.5 higher than at London. It must not, however, be inferred from these details, that the cold in winter, and the heat in summer, is never very extreme in England; for the climate varies nearly as much with respect to temp. as it does with respect to rain. It occasionally happens, that very great degrees of cold are felt during the winter months, especially towards the end of December and the beginning of January. In 1794, 1798, and 1813-14, the therm. was observed to be as low as within 5° of zero. The heat, on the contrary, is sometimes very excessive, especially in the S counties of England. In 1808, the therm., at many places in the vicinity of the metropolis, was nearly 90°. Winds.] It has already been remarked, in the general observations on the climate of this country, that the W and SW winds are much more prevalent, as well as constant, than the winds from any other quarter; and this circumstance may easily and naturally be accounted for, from the situation of the island, exposed to the Atlantic ocean. The W and SW winds are also the most violent, not only on the W side of the country, but likewse in the midland and eastern cos.; so much so, indeed, that the effects of their constancy and violence may generally be traced, in the evident inclination which they have given to the trees in all parts of the country. The winds that blow from the E and NE are, next to those that blow from the W and SW, the most regular and constant, and perhaps next to them the most violent; though it may be remarked, that in England the E and NE winds seldom blow violently for more than one or two days, whereas the W and SW winds frequently continue to blow violently for a considerably longer time. The wind that most seldom occurs, is that from the S. In some parts of the island, particularly in the W cos., SE winds are not uncommon. The NW winds do not blow for any considerable part of the year; but when they do blow, they are generally steady. Mr. Whitley remarks that, "the temp. and weather of the British isles are greatly influenced by the direction of the wind. On the S and W lies the Atlantic, sustaining on its bosom a reservoir of humid air. On the E lies the cold air of the Alps, or the dry air of the great northern plain, whose surface is covered with drift and gravel.
