When the wind blows for a few days in one direction, it covers this land with the atmosphere of the region which it has traversed, and impresses its distinctive character on our climate; in fact, the wind makes the weather. At Cork Dr. Smith states, that 'it appears from a regular diary of the weather, kept for several years in that city, that the winds blow from the S to the NW three-fourths of the year at least.' The general strike of the geological formations of these islands is SW and NE, which is in the direction of the prevailing winds. The range of the mountain-chains do not, therefore, afford that shelter which, under different circumstances, they would give; and of which Herefordshire and Cheshire, protected on the SW and NW by the Welsh hills, are good examples. The surface of Ireland is greatly exposed from this cause, and being mostly destitute of timber, the SW winds have a clear sweep over the island. In the SW of England there is a marked difference (indicated by the state of the timber) between the shelter in the secondary valleys which open to the west, and those on the E side of the water-shed line. The low E coast is fully exposed to a cold blighting wind from the NE in spring, generally in April or May, but sometimes continued till June. The NE wind is felt severely in Scotland in April and May, keeping back vegetation in spring, and shortening the cool summer. In Norfolk it is injurious to the fine stock, as well as the crops: and in Kent its blighting influence is further extended to the fruit blossom, which is often cut off. In addition to the low temperature of this wind, its dry exhaling character causes considerable damage." Barometrical pressure.] From the observations of Mr. Hutchinson at Liverpool, for 25 years, it was ascertained, that the mean height of the barometer was 29.74 in.; the greatest height during that period 30.95, the least 28.6; the greatest range 2.89, and the annual average range 196. The mean height in each month was as follows: January 29.71, February 29.58, March 29.80, April 29.78, May 29.80, June 29.82, July 29.82, August 29.81, September, 29.69, October 29.71, November 29.64, December 29.64. From this statement it is evident that the mean beight of the barom. in this part of the island is greater during the months of May, June, July, and August, than in any other four months.—The barom, at Dover was observed by Mr. Mantell, surgeon at that place, during the years 1789, 1790, 1791, 1792, and till September 1793. From his observations, it was found that the mean height of this instrument, on an average of 5 years, was 29-90 inches; the greatest height during that period 30.95; the least 28.48; the greatest range 2.47; and the mean annual range 1.80.—The mean height of the barom. at Kendal, collected from the observations of five years, beginning July 1787, was 29.7845. The greatest height of the barom at Keswick, during 1793, was 30.28; the least height 28.33; the mean height 29.55; the greatest range, 1.95; the mean monthly range 111. According to Mr. Dalton, the mean annual height at this place is 29.79.—The mean height of the barom, in London, upon an average of two observations in every day in the year, kept at the house of the Royal society, for many years, was found to be 2988. According to Mr. Kirwan, the usual variation of the barometer in England is 21 inches. Soils. The soils of E. are so various that it is impossible to give any general account of them; and in most parts of the kingdom they pass so frequently and suddenly from one kind to another, that it would be tedious, and far exceed the limits of this article, to describe them all, in the different districts. They may be classed under the following heads: clay, loam, sand, chalk, gravel, and peat. Of the first description there are two kinds,—deep, dark-coloured, rich clay; and clay of a less fertile quality, of a pale colour, and of comparatively small depth. The latter is by far the most prevalent in England. Of loams there are several varieties: strong loam, which in general has been formed of clay, long under a course of tillage, and consequently much exposed to the action of the sun and air; loam of a less tenacious nature; calcareous loam, and sandy loam; the last, like the first description of loam, is rather an artificial soil, formed of sand, enriched by manure, and long exposed to the action of the sun and air. Pure sand is found in few parts of E. to any extent, being for the most part changed into sandy loam; the | Perhaps the most splendid of all the herbaceous same may be said of chalk as a soil, it having, in most parts of the kingdom, received the nature and qualities of calcareous loam by cultivation. Of gravelly soils there are two kinds in England,—a yellow gravel, which in general is a hungry and unfertile soil, and a gravel of a dark colour, which, where not entirely or in a great measure denuded of earth, is by no means an unfertile soil. Peat or mossy soils are very common and extensive in the N district of England, and are also met with, though not so frequently, nor to such an extent, in the SW and S cos. Perhaps they are least common in the E and SE cos. There are very few instances where the same kind of soil extends for many miles without either being varied in its quality and appearance, or being actually changed into a different kind. Perhaps the wealds of Kent, Surrey, and Sussex, exhibit as large an extent of nearly the same kind of soil as any part of the kingdom, with the exception of Norfolk. There is also another kind of soil, which stretches to a great extent, though not always exactly of the same nature or quality, and occasionally broken in upon by a different description of soil: this is the red loam, which may be traced as far to the NE as Nottingham, abounds in Rutland, is not unfrequently met with in Northamptonshire, and appears thence to stretch to the SW into Gloucestershire and Devonshire.-With respect to Wales, both North and South, the soils in general, in the vales and low grounds, consist of loams of different degrees of fertility and strength. Some of the vales, especially in N. Wales, may vie in natural richness with the most favoured districts of E. The sea-coast of some parts of S. Wales consists of a sandy loam, which, from the moistness of the climate, is often very productive. The soil of the mountains is frequently thin and cold, but in some places dry and sound. The total extent of land, from the nature of the soil, or the elevation of the surface, incapable of cultivation, has been estimated at 3,256,000 acres in England, and 1,105,000 acres in Wales. Vegetable kingdom.] The flora of E., though it cannot boast the most splendid and exquisite of vegetable productions, yet contains as great a variety of genera and species as any other country of equal extent. The first for importance and variety is the family of grasses. Under almost all differences of soil and situation, we find the chief covering of the richest, as well as of the most barren tracts, made up for the most part of these plants. The most important grasses in meadows and pastures are the meadow foxtail grass, two or three species of hair-grass and meadow-grass, the cock's-foot fescue, and oat-grass. All the viviparous grasses, except one (Festuco vivipara), if transplanted to a lower and warmer situation, accommodate themselves to their new climate, and produce seeds. Nearly allied to the grasses in general habit are a number of species, natives of moors, bogs, and pools. These serve to give consistency to the deep mud or peat in which they are rooted, and when young, afford a coarse pasture to sheep and cattle. The leguminous or papilionaceous plants, so called from their winged blossoms, form a very important division in English botany. The herbage of all when fresh, and of many when dry, is a most grateful food to horses, cattle, and sheep; and several of them, as the clovers and vetches, are largely cultivated for this purpose. The umbelliferous plants form another large class in the natural arrangement of English vegetables. The roots and seeds of those kinds which grow on dry light soils, are frequently aromatic; those that are natives of marshes and moist meadows, are for the most part in a greater or less degree poisonous. plants are the bulbous rooted, which, from their general resemblance to the lily, have obtained the name of liliaceous; most of these, however, are natives of warmer climates. Of those which are found wild in E. there are only 28 species; and the greater number of these are of rare occurrence in a truly native state. The spring and autumnal crocus, the snow-drop, the snow-flake, the three kinds of narcissus, including the daffodil, the fritillary, tulip, and lily-of-the-valley, are more familiar to us as garden plants than as natives of our woods and pastures. The common ones of this class are ramsons, a species of garlic, meadow-saffron, and the beautiful and fragrant harebell or wild hyacinth, one of the principal ornaments of our groves and thickets, even at a time when they are profuse of beauties. Our native fruits belong for the most part to the class of rosaceous plants, such as the wood-strawberry, the bullace and black-thorn, the hawthorn, crab and mountain-ash, the common bramble or blackberry, the raspberry, stone-bramble, and cloudberry. The cherry, the medlar, the service, and pear-tree, whose fruit when wild is of so little account, belong also to this class. One of the largest of the natural classes of English vegetables is that of the radiated or compound flowered plants; but it is rather remarkable, that out of so large a number of plants, many of which are very abundant, and of great size, only a single one, the Tragoponon porifolius, should be applied to the sustenance of man, and not even a single one should be cultivated for the use of cattle. They occupy road-sides, ditch-banks, and all waste places that are incapable of cultivation; and seem peculiarly devoted to the sustenance of the granivorous birds by their seeds, and of numerous tribes of insects by their foliage. The sow-thistle, hawk-weed, bur-dock, thistle, cud-weed, coltsfoot, groundsel, dandelion, daisy, and yarrow, are the most commonly occurring genera. The most valuable of our native evergreens are the box, the pine, the yell, and the holly. Those of secondary consequence are the juniper and ivy, the spurge-laurel, the cranberry, and those extremely ornamental plants, the Vaccinium vitisidea, and Arbutus uva ursi. The deciduous timber-trees, that are either aboriginal or at least have been long naturalized to our soil, are the oak, the chestnut, and beech, all of which are moist bearing trees, or produce farinaceous oil nuts, the favourite food of hogs, and of many graminivorous quadrupeds; the birch, the alder, the horn-beam, the abele, the black-poplar, and the aspen, bearing catkins; the sycamore, the maple, and the ash; the lime, the elm, and the wych-hazel. A middle station between the timber-trees and shrubs is occupied by the hazel and the numerous species of willow. The pulpy fruit-bearing shrubs are the currant and gooseberry, the elder, the barberry, the bilberry, the cornel or dogwood, the buckthorn, the guelder-rose, and the mazereon. The ferns comprise a number of elegant plants that grow in moist, shady, and uncultivated places, the uses of which have been but little inquired into. The roots of most of them abound in a sweetish mucilage which in times of scarcity has been resorted to for nutriment. The larger kinds are collected and burned for the potash which is yielded from their ashes. The Equisetum hyemale is much used by turners and cabinet-makers, as a fine file to smooth their work with. Of marine algae, or sea-weeds, between 500 and 600 species are found upon our own shores. The more tender and gelatinous kinds are eaten, either raw or boiled; and the rest, on those rocky parts of the coast where they can be collected in great quantities, are occasionally burned into kelp for the use of the soap-boilers and glass-makers. Of cryptogamous plants there exist about 300 species. Animal kingdom.] The marsupial genera which first show the existence of mammalian life in England are extinct. All the species whose remains have been found in the oldest tertiary deposits, as the London clay, and the limestone of the isle of Wight, are now extinct. Of the quadrupeds that inhabited E. during the latest tertiary period, the following are now extinct:-The great elephant or mammoth, two species of rhinoceros, the hippopotamus, the great Irish deer, and another kind of deer of equal or superior bulk, the great cave-bear, the tiger, and the hyena, and a quadruped as large as the tiger but with larger and sharper tusks, a large species of beaver, and a large species of mole. These indeed are not known to exist at the present time in any part of the world. The following quadrupeds, which coexisted in E. with the above extinct species, have been extirpated in this island since the historical period, but still exist on the continent, viz., the common brown bear, the wolf, the beaver, the reindeer, and the aurochs. Most of the smaller or the useful quadrupeds which were introduced into E. with the mammoth, rhinoceros, &c., still exist with us; as, for example, the bat, the shrew, the mole, the badger, the otter, the pole-cat, weasel, fox, wild cat, the water-rat and field-rat, the hare, rabbit, horse, ass, short-horned ox, goat, stag, red deer, and roebuck. More than half of the species of mammalia that have been found fossilized in the newer tertiary formations and in many caves still exist. Of those which are extinct, some, as the largest carnivora and herbivora, apparently perished before the creation of man. Amongst the proofs that these actually lived and died, generation after generation, in E., may be adduced the great number of their fossil remains, and their perfect conservation in tranquil sedimentary lacustrine deposits; the striking evidence first brought to light by Dr. Buckland of limestone caves, as that at Kirkdale, having been long inhabited by hyenas, and the gnawed bones of young mammoths, rhinoceroses, and hippopotami, dragged into the caves by the hyenas, together with bones and shed antlers of gigantic extinct deer. The discovery of antlers, naturally shed, and with their branches entire, affords likewise very strong proof that the extinct deer lived on the spot where those antlers were found fossil. [Professor Owen.] Among the most important of the existing race of animals is the English horse, which has been greatly improved by crossing with the finest foreign breeds, till in spirit, strength, and speed, he is fully equal or perhaps superior to that of any other country. The different breeds of sheep, too, have been greatly improved by the care and skill of the breeder. Dogs of every variety have been naturalized; but the bulldog is said to be peculiarly English, and it possesses strength and courage in an extraordinary degree. Of savage animals, since the extirpation of the wolf, the largest and strongest are the fox and wild cat. The badger is frequently met with, as also the stoat, the martin-of which there are two species-the otter, the squirrel, and the dormouse. Rats are numerous, particularly the brown rat of India, falsely called the Norway rat, which has nearly extirpated the native iron-grey rat. Mice of various kinds are very common. The hedgehog is not rare, and the mole is a nuisance in every rich and well-cultivated field. The stag is yet found in its native state upon the borders of Cornwall, and two species of fallow-deer are still preserved. Hares are abundant. The sea-calf and great seal are frequently seen upon the coasts, particularly the coast of Wales.—The larger birds of prey have now almost everywhere disappeared, as indeed they generally do from a country well-cultivated and well-inhabited; but the golden eagle is still found on Snowdon in Wales, and the black eagle is sometimes seen in Derbyshire. The osprey or seaeagle seems to be extinct. The peregrine, or common falcon, is confined to Wales; but the various kinds of hawks are numerous all over the country. The largest wild bird is the bustard; it occurs, but rarely, in the E. cos., and weighs from 25 to 27 lbs.; the smallest is the golden-crested wren, which sports in the branches of the loftiest pines. The nightingale, celebrated for its plaintive tones and extraordinary compass of voice, is confined chiefly to the eastern and middle cos., and is rarely observed to the N of Doncaster. The domestic birds of E. seem to be wholly of foreign origin: the poultry from Asia, the Guinea-fowl from Africa, the peacock from India, the pheasant from Colchis in Asiatic Turkey, and the turkey from America.-The English reptiles are the frog, the toad, a species of tortoise, lizards of several kinds, and serpents, some of which have been found 4 ft. in length. The viper alone is venomous.—On the coasts are taken turbot, dace, sole, cod, plaice, smelt, mullet, pilchards, and herrings; the basking-shark sometimes occurs on the Welsh coasts. The river-fish are the salmon, trout, pike, char, greyling, samlet, tench, perch, and many other kinds. Various parts of the coast afford shellfish of different species. The most esteemed oysters are the green oyster from Colchester in Essex, and the white oyster from Milton in Kent. Geological structure.] If a line be drawn from the W side of the isle of Portland, passing through Dorsetshire, about half-way between Dorchester and Bridport, and running a few miles to the W of Oxford and Northampton, and a very short distance to the E of Leicester and Nottingham, afterwards inclining rather to the W, so as to include Doncaster and York between it and the German ocean, and at length sweeping round the last town, in a direction nearly NE, so as to reach the vicinity of Scarborough, it will include between it and the German ocean the whole of the low district of England, which is composed of chalk, calcareous sandstone, and other secondary strata or alluvial ground, and in which no beds of workable coal or metallic veins occur. To the W of this imaginary line the country is composed of secondary strata of a different description, in many parts of which beds of ironstone and coal are found. This Mr. Bakewell denominates the middle district. On the N this district is bounded by mountains of metalliferous limestone, which, entering from Scotland, proceed nearly through the centre of England, and terminate in Derbyshire; the same species of country likewise makes its appearance in the SW cos. and in Wales. The primary and transition mountains, in which metallic ores occur, are met with along the W side of the island. These constitute the alpine parts of England, extending from Cornwall and Devonshire through Wales, into the NW parts of Yorkshire and Lancashire, and through Westmoreland and Cumberland. It may be remarked, however, that the calcareous strata of the low district appear on some parts of the E side of Durham and Northumberland, beyond Whitby. The principal coal-fields lie in that part of the middle district which stretches from Derbyshire to Northumberland, and in that part of Wales which borders on the Bristol channel. On the W side of Cumberland the coal strata border on a small part of the alpine district, and dip under the sea. In the centre and E of England the strata generally decline to the SE; on the W side they are more broken and irregular. In a direction SE from Nantwich in Cheshire to Worcester, all the rock-salts, and most of the brine-springs, are situated. Granite and granitic rocks occur in Cornwall, Devonshire, N. Wales, Anglesea, the Malvern-hills in Worcestershire, Charnwood-forest in Leicestershire, and in Cumberland and Westmoreland. The granite of these two cos. is, however, porphyritic; that of Charnwood is nearly allied to syenite. Blocks of granite are found detached in some parts of Lancashire and Cheshire. Gneiss is found nowhere in England except at the Malvern-hills, and here it is imperfectly formed. Mica slate occurs only in Cornwall, where | borough. The vitriolated chalybeate, as the Hartfell along with it, and lying over granite, serpentine, another primary rock, is found exclusively, with the exception of the isle of Anglesea.—Of the intermediate or transition rocks, slate is found in Westmoreland, Yorkshire, Leicestershire, N. Wales, Cornwall, and Devonshire. In the Yorkshire slate are cubic crystals of pyrites. In the slate rock at Charnwoodforest in Leicestershire, the slaty laminæ make an angle of 60° with the principal seam by which the rock is divided. Greywacke is met with in Cornwall, where it is called killas. In the N part of Radnorshire is a lofty range of mountains composed of greywacke, which on the E side resembles sandstone, and on the W roof-slate.—The compact limestone of England and Wales belongs properly to the transition rocks. In Yorkshire and the N cos. it rests on slate, and contains a greater quantity of organic remains than the Devonshire limestone. Trap or greenstone is met with in Cornwall in transition rocks; but it possesses no characters by which it can be distinguished from that found in primary rocks.—Transitions from granite to syenite and greenstone occur in the same block at Charnwood-forest. The two latter are also met with in Cornwall, Wales, and Cumberland. Amygdaloid, provincially called toadstone, is found in great abundance in Derbyshire. Porphyry occurs on the W side of England and Wales. Porphyritic greenstone is scattered over the N side of Cader-Idris. Basalt is found in Durham, Shropshire, and several other localities.—The lowest of the secondary rocks in England is frequently a silicious sandstone coloured by oxide of iron, and called red sandstone. The secondary rocks also include argillaceous sandstone, earthy limestone, calcareous sandstone, and chalk; and contain besides the rock-salt, gypsum, iron-stone, coal, and basalt. On the E side of England the coal strata generally decline to the SE; on the W side they are frequently thrown into different and opposite directions by faults and dykes. The deepest coal-mines in E. are those of Northumberland and Durham, some of which are wrought more than 800 yds. below the surface. The thickest bed of English coal is in Staffordshire, where it attains a thickness of 30 ft. In general, the beds of coal in other parts do not exceed from 6 to 9 ft. in thickness.—With respect to the upper secondary rocks, and the strata containing organic remains in E., there are magnesian limestone, chalk, flint, gypsum, the marle and sand over which in many parts contain a large quantity of red oxide of iron. Gypsum is principally found in Cheshire, Shropshire, Worcestershire, Derbyshire, and Nottinghamshire. In the gypsum of the two latter cos. no organic remains have been discovered. The organic remains found in the clay and gravel which cover the chalk in the SE cos. have already been noticed. The gravel over the clay is composed principally of flint, containing distinct impressions of unknown aquatic animals. Between the clay and the gravel there is sometimes a thin layer of fresh-water shells, which are also met with under the clay. In the strata over chalk, in the isle of Wight, fresh-water shells have lately been detected. Mineral waters.] A brief notice of the principal mineral waters of E. seems properly to belong to this part of our subject. They are thus classed by Dr. Saunders: The simpler thermal, viz., Bristol, the temp. of which is 74°; Matlock, the temp. of which is 66°; and Buxton, with a temp. of 82°. The simple saline, of which Epsom is the principal. The simple carbonated chalybeate, of which Tunbridge is the principal. The hot carbonated chalybeate, or Bath waters, the temp. of which is 116°. The saline carbonated chalybeate, as the Cheltenham and Scarwaters: and the cold sulphureous, as the Harrowgate. [ The waters of Malvern and Holywell seem to owe their medicinal virtues entirely to their purity. Deer, as free as in an American forest, wandered there by thousands. The fox, whose life is in many counties held almost as sacred as that of a human being, was considered as a mere nuisance. The wild bull, with his white mane, was still to be found wandering in a few of the southern forests. The badger made his dark and tortuous hole on the side of every hill where copse-wood grew thick. The wild cats were frequently heard by night wailing round the lodges of the rangers of Whittlebury and Needwood. The yellow-breasted martin was still pursued in Cranbourne-chase for his fur, reputed inferior only to that of the sable. Fen eagles, measuring more than 9 ft. between the extremities of the wings, preyed on fish along the coast of Norfolk. On all the downs, from the British channel to Yorkshire, huge bustards strayed, in troops of 50 or 60, and were often hunted with greyhounds. The marshes of Cambridgeshire and Lincolnshire were covered, during some months of every year, by immense clouds of cranes. Some of the races the progress of cultivation has extirpated; of others the numbers are so much diminished that men crowd to gaze at a specimen as at a Bengal tiger, or a Polar bear." [Gardener's Chronicle.] divided into six districts, or distinct tracts, each of in grazing, sheep-farming, arable-management, and mixed cultivation. 1. The northern district includes the principal parts of Northumberland and Durham, the whole of Cumberland, Westmoreland, Lancashire, and Yorkshire, (excepting the fens and marshes bordering on Lincolnshire,) with parts of Cheshire, Staffordshire, and Derbyshire. This district is distinguished by coolness of climature, and backwardness of seasons, as compared with the more S parts of the island; but its most striking natural feature is derived from its mountains. As a field of rural economy, likewise, it is strongly distinguished from the other agricultural districts. On its W side manufactures indeed have prevailed over agriculture; but on its E side all the branches of the latter art flourish. It would not indeed be easy to point out any portion of the kingdom in which a greater degree of agricultural skill and industry is displayed than in the N parts of Northumberland, and on the banks of the Tees. 2. The western district extends from the Mersey to the banks of the Somersetshire Avon; being bounded on the W, by the Welsh mountains; on the E, by the lower hills of Staffordshire, and the uplands of Warwickshire and Oxfordshire; and on the S, by the chalk-hills of Wiltshire, and the Sedgemoors of Somersetshire. Nearly the whole of this division comprises an uninterrupted succession of vale-districts, formed by the passage of the Severn, the Avon, the Dee, and the Mersey. to the sea. It is no less distinctly marked by its agricultural productions: as the whole of it, with the exception of the high lands of Shropshire and Herefordshire, the Cotswold-hills in Gloucestershire, and the Mendip-hills in Somersetshire, may be said to be applied to the produce of the dairy. It is also distinguished as an agricultural district by its fruit-liquor. 3. The midland district is bounded by the mountains of the N, and the chalk-hills of the S district, in its length; and by the rising grounds, which separate it from the W division, and the banks of the marshes, where the E division commences, in its breadth. Compared with the great variety of soil and surface exhibited by the other divisions, this may be regarded as one widely extended plain of fertile lands, without a single eminence, except the Charnwood-hills. In its agricultural character, it is distinguished by its mixed cultivation, to which the nature of its soil and surface is almost uniformly suitable. "As a wide field of agriculture, in which every branch of the profession is highly cultivated, it has been long popularly known. Here not only the spirit of improvement, but of enterprise, may be said to inhabit. The art, science, and mystery of breeding has here been carried to a height which in any other country probably it has never attained; the same enterprising spirit, which led to this preeminence, still continuing, with little or any abatement." [Marshall.] 4. The eastern district is not more strongly marked in its natural than in its agricultural character. In the former, its fens and marshes, as well as the light sandy quality of its uplands—features that are united in no other district of the kingdom—mark it out; in the latter, the turnip-husbandry is the most distinguishing feature. The agricultural industry of this division is directed chiefly to grazing, not only in the marshes and lower grounds but on the uplands. Sheep as well as cattle are grazed; and it may be remarked that in a very considerable portion of this agricultural district, arable husbandry is extensively and intimately connected with the fattening of sheep and cattle. Its boundaries are distinctly marked, including the fen-lands of Lincolnshire, Northamptonshire, Cambridgeshire, Huntingdonshire, and Norfolk, as well as the rest of Norfolk, and the cos. of Suffolk and Essex, with those parts of the adjoining cos. which lie close to them. 5. The southern district, which includes the chalkhills in the vicinity of the metropolis, as well as those in more remote cos., is by these hills distinctly marked out in its natural character from the rest of the kingdom. That part of it which lies in the more immediate vicinity of the metropolis is of course directed, in the objects of its husbandry, by the demands created by the metropolis; and thus an artificial character is, as it were, given to its agricultural pursuits. The other part of this district is distinguished by the flocks of sheep which are fed on its chalk-hills; and the breeds, and mode of management, of which are very different from those in the midland and eastern divisions. 6. The natural situation of the last, or south-western district, is remarkable. It stretches away from the main body of the island, in a peninsular form, into the western sea. This peninsula is nearly 200 m. long, and is bounded by that sea, except where it touches the southern and western divisions. The natural characters of its area, as well as of its situation, are also singular. Slate-rock hills, which are comparatively unknown in the rest of the kingdom, except in a small portion of the northern department, here abound. The surface indeed, almost throughout, its NE angle excepted, is of a singular character, consisting of bare steep-sided hills, separated by narrow valleys. The hills, in general, are productive to their very summits. In its agricultural character and pursuits it is not less remarkable; the system of husbandry that is carried on here being more nearly allied, it is supposed, to the ancient husbandry of the Romans than any followed in other districts of this island. Arable as well as pasture and dairy husbandry are indeed pursued here, but the mode of uniting them, and the practices which prevail in each, are remarkable, and strongly distinguished. The following are the principal cos. in which arable husbandry is pursued to a greater extent than in the other parts of the kingdom: Kent, Essex, Suffolk, Norfolk, Hampshire, Berkshire, Bedfordshire, Surrey, Sussex, Hertfordshire, part of Yorkshire, Durham, and Northumberland. The dairycos., for butter or cheese, or both, are Cheshire. Shropshire, Gloucestershire, Wiltshire, Buckinghamshire, Essex, Suffolk, Yorkshire, Derbyshire, Cambridgeshire, Dorsetshire, and Devonshire. The cos. most distinguished for breeding and fattening cattle and sheep are Lincolnshire, Somersetshire, Leicestershire, Northamptonshire, Teeswater in Durham, and Cleveland and Holderness in Yorkshire. It will be evident, however, that in this arrangement many cos. must be specified in which more than one of the three great agricultural pursuits are carried on; and it may also be remarked, that the arable husbandry, in some cos., is closely connected with the fattening of sheep, but seldom with the dairy husbandry; while the latter is often connected with the breeding and fattening of cattle and sheep. Hops.] England has long been noted for its hops. They are cultivated in the neighbourhood of Canterbury and Maidstone in Kent, where there is a larger extent of ground under this crop than in any other county in the kingdom; about Farnham in Surrey, and the adjoining parts of Hampshire, where, from superior management, or other causes, their quality is so highly esteemed that they always bring a higher price than any other hops; in Worcestershire, particularly in the vale of the Severn and in the vale of the Team; in the vicinity of Stowmarket in Suffolk, and near Retford in Nottinghamshire, but in these two places only to a very inconsiderable extent; in Herefordshire, particularly on the confines of Worcestershire; in Essex, about Castle-Hedingham; and in some parts of Sussex. This crop is so much affected by the climate, and so much exposed to blights and other distempers, that its produce varies more perhaps than that of any other plant cultivated in the fields of England. This will sufficiently appear, from the following statement of the produce of hops in England during the years 1794 and 1795. In the former year, it amounted only to 5,000,000 lbs., whereas in 1795, the produce was 42,528,587 lbs. Even within the last two years, while the duty payable in 1848-at the rate of 18s. 8d. per cwt., with an additional 5 per cent -amounted to nearly £400,000; in 1849, it was somewhat less than £200,000. The average, ascertained from the produce of a considerable number of years, was found to be 20,543,070 lbs. about 1806; it is now about 30,000,000 lbs. Hop grounds let at a higher rate per acre than any other kind of land not possessed of peculiar local advantages, bringing from £5 to £10 per acre; and about Farnham considerably more. Their produce has varied, on the same ground, between the years 1835 and 1844, from 15 cwt. to 3 cwt. per acre. Orchards.] There is scarcely a farm of any extent in the S and SW cos., which has not an orchard attached to it, containing apple-trees at least sufficiently numerous and productive to supply the farmer's family with cider; but it is exclusively or principally in the cos. of Devonshire, Herefordshire, Worcestershire, Gloucestershire, Monmouthshire, and Somersetshire, that cider and perry are made in large quantities for sale, and the management of the orchard forms principally or exclusively the concern of the farmer. Perry is made chiefly in Worcestershire; the cider of Herefordshire and Gloucestershire is deemed of a superior quality to that of Devonshire. The actual quantity of cider made in the cider-cos, has not been ascertained. Mr. Marshall calculates that the produce of the four cos. of Worcester, Gloucester, Hereford, and Monmouth, on a par of years, may be laid at 30,000 hds.; of this quantity it is supposed Worcestershire supplies nearly 10,000 hds.; besides 1,000 hds. of perry. Exclusively of these liquors, which are sent into all parts of the kingdom, these cos. send large quantities of fruit chiefly into the N cos. Kent is famous for its orchards of cherries and filberts. The former are common in most parts of Kent; the latter are principally confined to the vicinity of Maidstone, where they occupy several hundred acres. Sheep.] Sheep are fed not only on the mountainous districts is to prepare them for the butcher. In some parts they are kept on arable farms, not only for the purpose of being fattened, but also to manure the fallows by being folded on them. The principal varieties of sheep kept in this country are, the Dishley or New Leicester, the Lincoln, the Teeswater, the Dartmoor, the Exmoor, the Dorset, the Hereford, the South-Down, the Norfolk, the Heath, the Hardwick, the Cheviot, the Dunfaced, the Romney-Marsh, the Wiltshire, the Bagshot-Heath, and the Spanish or Merino. The two breeds which at present chiefly engage the attention of English farmers are the New Leicester and the improved South-Down. The fleece of the former, from its great length and weight, is highly valuable; but the animal requires rich and sheltered pasturage. The latter is a hardy breed, and capable of living on short pasture; but is not sufficiently hardy to endure the severities of the Cheviot hills, or the Welsh mountains. The Cheviot sheep are best-adapted to their native hills, and all other pastures of a similar character. The breeds of sheep are distinguished from one another both by the length of the staple and the mode of manufacturing it; the one being adapted to the fabrication of woollen goods, and the other to that of worsted. Long-woolled sheep are found in many detached parts but much more commonly on the E than the W side of England, and generally nearer to the coast than the middle of the kingdom. Amongst the larger ranges of long-woolled sheep, the most northern are found near the mouth of the Tees; the next range are found in that district which comprehends the SE point of Yorkshire, nearly the whole of Lincolnshire, and the fen lands of Huntingdon, Cambridge, and Norfolk. The same kind of sheep, with respect to wool, are likewise found in the smaller marshes of Essex and of Kent; but in the latter co. they are most numerous in the marshes of Romney and Guilford. Long-woolled sheep are also found in Dorset, Devon, and Cornwall, on the Cotswold-hills, in some small and detached parts of Lancashire, Oxfordshire, Bedfordshire, and Staffordshire, through the whole of Leicestershire, Rutlandshire, Northamptonshire, and Huntingdonshire, and along the banks of most of the large rivers in the kingdom.—The short-woolled sheep are of six different kinds: viz., the Norfolk, South-Down, Wiltshire, Ryeland or Herefordshire, the Heath-sheep, and the Cheviot or mountain-sheep. None of our short-wool breeds furnish wool of a quality that can be worked up with advantage, without a mixture of wool of a softer quality grown in foreign countries; but no foreign combing-wool is introduced that comes into competition with the produce of the English long-woolled breeds; indeed, almost no combing-wool is imported except what comes from our own colonies. English long wool is used for the finer class of carpets, moreens, damask, hosiery, lastings, and the coarse stuff-goods sold at Bradford and Halifax. Over nearly the whole of Wales the sheep are singularly small, with horns, white faces, and white legs; but the influence of different kinds of manufactures is visible on the flocks of the principality, especially on the flocks of Montgomery, over which the action of the market at Welshpool for Welsh flannels extends, In Glamorganshire, a breed somewhat similar to those on the Cotswold-hills is kept. In N. Wales the stock is light, 5 acres supporting not more than a single sheep, exclusive of cattle. In this division of Wales it may be estimated that 683,000 are maintained, producing nearly 5,300 packs of wool, the fleece being reckoned on an average at 2 lbs. The principal marts for it are the fairs of Llanrwst and Bangor. On the mountains of S. Wales the same kind of sheep are met with, but they are more numerous in general. Their fleeces are reckoned at 11 lb.; and give as their total weight 3,570 packs. the produce of 571,000 sheep. The home manufactures take off the whole of it. Besides the various kinds of sheep which have been described, the Merino or Spanish sheep were for some years a very favourite breed; but it has been ascertained, that though their fleece does not much degenerate here, yet the carcase-which is naturally ill-formed, and affords comparatively little weight of meatdoes not improve; and as the farmer, in the kind of sheep which he keeps, must look not only to the produce of wool, but also to the butcher-market, he has found it his interest rather to keep to the native breeds of his own country. Merinos, however, have been of considerable service to the flocks of E., having been judiciously and successfully crossed in many instances with the South-Down, Ryeland, &c. Taking all kinds of sheep, and the two different kinds of fleeces, the average stock per acre in England is 59375 sheep; the average fleece nearly 4 lbs. 8 oz.; the average produce of long skinwool per acre about 5 oz.; of short skin-wool 4 oz.; and of skinwool over the whole kingdom nearly 5 oz .- The total number of sheep in England and Wales, with perhaps as near an approximation to the truth as could be expected on such a subject, was estimated as follows in 1814: Total number of sheep and lambs, Mr. Luccock, in his Treatise on English Wool, which we have followed through all these details, was of opinion that the estimate of 600,000 packs of wool as the annual produce of England and Wales, was far above the truth, and that it did not reach 400,000 packs. It is not now estimated to exceed 470,000 packs of 240 lbs. each. Cattle.] The breeds and varieties of cattle at present reared on English farms are exceedingly numerous, and approximate one another by a series of the nicest and almost imperceptible gradations. Yet they can be comprehensively distributed into five great groups; -the polled or hornless, in Suffolk and Norfolk; the crumpled-horned, in Alderney and some parts of the S of England; the short-horned, in Durham, E. Yorkshire, Lincolnshire, and the northern cos.; the middle-horned, in Herefordshire, Gloucestershire, N. Devon, E. Sussex, and Wales; and the longhorned, in Lancashire and in the midland cos. But only in their native districts, or on a few select estates, are these to be found pure; everywhere else they are so thoroughly and intricately intermixed as to form a countless and bewildering number of mongrel varieties. Mr. Youatt assigns the long-horns to Ireland, and claims the middle-horns as the aboriginal British. "Difference of climate gradually wrought some change, and particularly in their bulk. The rich pasture of Sussex fattened the ox of that district into his superior size and weight. The plentiful but not so luxuriant herbage of the N of Devon, produced a somewhat smaller and more active animal, while the occasional privations of Wales lessened the bulk and thickened the hide of the Welsh runt." The slightest observation will convince us that the cattle in Devonshire, Sussex, Wales, and Scotland, are essentially the same. They are middle-horned; tolerable but not extraordinary milkers, and remarkable for the quality rather than the quantity of their milk; active at work; and with an unequalled aptitude to fatten. They have all the characters of the same breed, changed by soil and climate and time; yet little changed by the intermeddling of man." There are no data on which to found an estimate nearly approaching to the truth respecting the number of cattle kept in England. Mr. Arthur Young, in his Tour to the North of England in 1802, estimated the total value of cattle at £36,480,000; and as at that period the average value of cattle per head could not be more than £10, the whole number kept then was probably between 3,000,000 and 4,000,000; the stock since that time has not increased much.— The rearing of calves forms an important branch of husbandry in some parts, particularly in Essex, and in the vale of Aylesbury in Buckinghamshire. In Cheshire, more calves are fed during the months of March and April than in any other part of E.; but as the milk cannot be long spared, they are killed very young. Horses.] There are several kinds of horses bred and kept in this country. In the midland cos., particularly in Northamptonshire and Leicestershire, a very large breed is kept, generally of a dark black colour, and well-adapted for the cart. Yorkshire also has long been celebrated for its breed of horses, both for the saddle and for draught, but especially for the former. The Welsh horses are small but active. In no other country in the world has equal attention been paid to the rearing of horses for hunting and the race-course; and this attention has been rewarded by the unrivalled excellence of the animals reared.-The number of horses employed in agriculture has never been very accurately ascertained. Mr. Marshall supposes that each square mile employs 20 horses, or about 30 to every 100 acres. If this supposition be correct, and we reckon that in England and Wales there are 30,000,000 acres of cultivated ground, the number of horses would be about 900,000. In 1804, the number of horses for which duty was paid in Great Britain was 1,178,000; but besides these, it was calculated that at this period there were 200,000 pleasure-horses, 40,000 cavalry horses, 1,200,000 employed in husbandry, and 350,000 colts and mares - Mules and asses, especially the former, are not abundant in E.; and are scarcely if at all used in husbandry. They are in general larger than those of other countries, Spain, Portugal, and Malta excepted. Timber.] In enumerating the agricultural products of E., timber must not be forgotten. In the S, and particularly in most of the SW and W cos., the quantity of timber which grows in the hedge-rows or in the pasture-fields is very great. In some parts of E., also, much timber grows in woods and plantations; so that, taking the whole of E., it is calculated that on each estate the value of the growing timber is equal to two years' rent. The principal woodland cos. are Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire, Worcestershire, Cheshire, and parts of Oxfordshire, Northamptonshire, Berkshire, Leicestershire, &c. It may in general be remarked that the W cos. are better wooded than the E, and the S than the N. It is calculated that in Worcestershire alone there are in the enclosures between 300,000 and 400,000 trees, principally oak, ash, elm, and willow, which might be removed without any sensible injury either to the proprietor or the occupier of the soil. Oak is by far the most important, though not the most abundant of English trees. It flourishes in great perfection on the wealds of Kent, Surrey, and Sussex; but the principal supply is derived from the royal forests, of which the following statement will show the extent of the land in which the timber belongs to the Crown: Acres. In New-forest, Mines and Quarries. It has been justly observed that "it seldom or never happens that countries abundant in the productions of agriculture should, at the same time, present an opulent mineralogy:" yet England is far from being deficient in this respect, and holds a distinguished place among European nations, not so much perhaps on account of the very great abundance and natural richness of its minerals, as by reason of the industry, skill, and enterprise with which she has availed herself of these treasures.—Coal, it may in general be remarked, abounds in all parts of the kingdom, except the S, SE, SW, and a few of the midland cos. In the following cos., it is found in greater or less abundance: Northumberland, Durham, Yorkshire, Cumberland, and Lancashire, in the N of England. In this district, Westmoreland is the only co. destitute of coal; in all the rest it is very abundant. To the S of these cos., coal is found in Derbyshire, Staffordshire, and Shropshire: it is wanting in Cheshire on the W side, and Nottinghamshire and Lincolnshire on the E side of this district. Of the midland cos., Leicestershire and Warwickshire are the only two which possess this valuable mineral, none being found in Northamptonshire, Rutlandshire, Oxfordshire, or indeed to the S or E of these cos.; so that, besides the cos. enumerated in these directions, Huntingdonshire, Cambridgeshire, Norfolk, Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, Kent, Surrey, Sussex, Hertfordshire, Hampshire, Buckinghamshire, Bedfordshire, and Berkshire, are destitute of coal. It is also wanting in Wiltshire, Dorsetshire, Worcestershire, Herefordshire, Devonshire, (with the exception of the bovey coal,) and Cornwall; Gloucestershire, Somersetshire, and Monmouthshire, being the only cos. that possess coal-mines in this portion of England. With respect to N. Wales, coal is found in Flintshire and Denbighshire; and in S. Wales, in Pembrokeshire, Caermarthenshire, and Glamorganshire.—Iron is found in Cornwall, Cumberland, Derbyshire, Devonshire, Durham, Gloucestershire, Hampshire, Kent, Lancashire, Monmouthshire, Northumberland, Shropshire, Somersetshire, Staffordshire, Sussex, Warwickshire, and Wiltshire; but it is most abundant in Shropshire, Gloucestershire, Derbyshire, the N of Lancashire, and Wales. Lead is found principally in Derbyshire, Cumberland, Northumberland, Somersetshire, Devonshire, Yorkshire, Durham, Westmoreland, Cardigan, Flint, and Montgomery.-Tin is confined to Cornwall and the adjoining parts of Devonshire; and black lead to a small district in Cumberland. Mines of copper are wrought in Cornwall, Devonshire, Derbyshire, and Anglesea, and partially in Yorkshire, Staffordshire, &c.: zinc is met with in Derbyshire, Cornwall, &c.: manganese on the Mendip-hills in Somersetshire; rock-salt in Cheshire and Worcestershire; alum-slate in Yorkshire; gypsum or alabaster in Derbyshire and Nottinghamshire; fuller's earth in Berkshire, Bedfordshire, and Surrey; potter's clay principally in Dorsetshire. The stone - quarries most celebrated for the abundance and excellence of their stone, are those of Portland, Purbeck, Bath, Yorkshire, Northamptonshire, &c. The most extensive quarries of slate are in Westmoreland, Yorkshire, Leicestershire, N. Wales, Cornwall, and Devonshire. The profits from quarries throughout England and Wales were returned for the year ending 5th April 1811 at £29,160; and for that ending 5th April 1843, at £207,000. Coal-mines ] It is difficult to form an estimate of the quantity don the consumption is nearly about a ton annually for each individual, or perhaps more correctly about 3 chaldrons (Winton measure, or 28 cwt.) for each house. In Manchester, where the climate is colder, and coals cheaper, it is calculated that each individual uses 2 tons annually. In Newcastle, where the weather is still colder, and coals cheaper, the mean consumption is onethird of a Newcastle chaldron (nearly 18 cwt.) per month for every constant fire, equalizing the kitchen-fire with the rest; and reckoning 11 fire through the year, 10.5 chaldrons will be the annual consumption of each house. This perhaps may be taken as the extreme consumption of coals in families; and supposing the consumption in London to be at the lowest rate, the medium will be 6.75 chaldrons for each house. This, it must be confessed, is mere conjecture, but it is probably not far from the truth. We shall not even offer a conjecture of the quantity of coals used in our manufactories, salt-works, iron-works, and upon our railways, in all of which the consumption of coal must be immense. The declared value of coals and culm exported in 1848 was £1,088,221; in 1849, £1,088,148. The quantity of mineral fuel shipped coastwise for all the ports of Great Britain in 1818 was 3,450,000 tons; in 1828 it was 4,500,000 tons; and in 1844, 7,377,000 tons. In 1847, the quantity exported from Newcastle and Sunderland alone was little short of 4,500,000 tons. The cos. of Northumberland and Durham are richer in the article of coal than any other portion of the British isles; and fully 33,000 hands are employed in the coal-works in these two cos. alone. The coal-seams of this district lie in the form of a basin or trough, extending in length about 60 m.; in breadth, about 25 m. In some parts the coal protrudes from the surface; it has been wrought to the depth of 822 ft. In a Report of the South Shields committee on Accidents in Coal-mines, it is observed, that it is an erroneous basis of calculation to assume the entire area of the coal-fields as disposable to this country. Mr. Conybeare believes the Yorkshire and Derbyshire coal-field to rival or surpass in importance that of the N of England; which latter another writer [Lardner's Cyclopædia— Fossil Fuel] makes extend over 1,500 sq. m., but omits to state that on an average, even in the most productive measures, not more than 29 ft. of workable coal, by pits often 200 fath. down, in which upwards of 36 per cent. is left behind, can be procured; that some of the most valuable seams crop out towards the magnesian limestone; and that a vast proportion of this coal-field is removed by distance, and other natural obstructions, beyond the reach of remunerative employment of capital and labour. Other geologists [Philosoph. Trans., vol. xcvi, and Bakewell's Geology] make the S. Welsh basin about 1,000 sq. m. of 95 ft. of coal, containing 100,000 tons per acre, or 64,000,000 tons per sq. m., or almost as much as would supply the country at its present rate of consumption for 3,000 years! "Here again," says the Report of the South Shields committee, "it has not been stated that of this there is nearly one-half waste; and that much of this Welsh coal is inferior anthracite, which is only applicable for certain purposes; and that the cost of production, of even the finest description, has hitherto prevented its meeting in successful competition, either in the home or foreign markets, the fine coal of the Tyne and other districts." On the other hand, Professor Sedgewick is "convinced that with the present increased and increasing demand for coal, 400 years will leave little more than the name of our best seams;" and so thinks Professor Buckland. While Bakewell in his Geology observes, "We cannot but regard the exhaustion of our coal-beds as involving the destruction of a great portion of our private comfort and national prosperity: nor is the period very remote when the coal-districts which at present supply the metropolis with fuel will cease to yield any more. The number and extent of the principal coal-beds in Northumberland and Durham are known; and from these data it has been calculated that the coal in these cos. will last 360 years. The only coal-fields of any extent on the E side of England, between London and Durham, are those of Derbyshire and the W. Riding of Yorkshire. The Derbyshire coal-field is not of sufficient magnitude to supply, for any long period, more than is required for the home-consumption and that of the adjacent cos. There are very valuable beds of coal in the eastern part of the W. Riding of Yorkshire, which are yet unwrought; but the time is not very far distant when they must be put in requisition to supply the vast demand, of that manufacturing country, which at present consumes nearly all the produce of its own coal-mines. In the midland cos., Staffordshire possesses the nearest coal-district to the metropolis of any great extent, but such is the immense daily consumption of coal in the iron-furnaces and foundries, that it is generally believed this will be the first of our own coal-fields that will be exhausted. The 30 ft. bed of coal in the Dudley coal-field is of limited extent; and in the present mode of working it more than two-thirds of the coal is wasted and left in the mine. If we look to Whitehaven, or Lancashire, or to any of the minor coalfields in the W of England, we can derive little hope of their being able to supply London and the southern cos. with coal after the import fails from Northumberland and Durham. We may thus anticipate a period not very remote, when all the English mines of coal and ironstone will be exhausted." The following details are offered by the South Shields committee in support of these views:—Of 45 ft. of coal in a section of all the coal-strata in one of the best pits on the Tyne, not 30 ft. are workable coal. In the N, coal in the mine cannot be extracted to profit under 2 ft. 8 in., as in the Yard coal-seam; and in the W part of Wallsend the Bensham seam is considered unworkable at 2 ft. 10 in, alwrought in Yorkshire is about 13 in.; in N Lancashire, 20 in.; at Shilbottle, in the Northumberland and Durham districts, 2 ft. 6 in.; at St. Lawrence, 2 ft. 9 in.; at Wylam, Walbottle, Charlaw, &c., it is 3 ft. 2 in. Most of the thin seams above specified as in and commercial purposes. The northern mines, it is thus seen, notwithstanding their facilities of market and carriage, prove working whenever the seam falls to 2 ft. 8 or 2 ft. 10 in.; and coal even of a much greater thickness, unconnected with other seams, would not repay the cost of deep pits and expensive works. Already the Tyne portion of the N coal-field, hitherto considered tion in the finer description of coal; and that of the Wear and the Tees, less worked than it, has the advantage in commercial competition of from 9 to 10 per cent. in price. For several years past in the London market the Wear and Tees coal brings regularly a higher price by at least 2s. per ton than that of the Tyne. When the expense of working British coal-mines shall leave no remuneration for capital and labour employed, when brought into competition with the mines of other countries, then will they be as effectually lost to Britain as if no longer in physical existence; and her superiority in the mechanic arts and manufactures, ceteris paribus, it may well be feared, will be superseded. The more extensive and easily accessible coal-fields, both bituminous and anthracite, of the United States of America, which are to the W of the Alleghany mountains, 1,500 m. long, and 600 m. broad, and the fine anthracite combined with bituminous coal of Pennsylvania, running on the E of these mountains as far as Lake Ontario, afford supplies of the finest coal that time would almost seem incapable of exhausting, which are at present only deterred from coming into competition by the want of capital and cheap labour. The N parts of France and Belgium are rich in fine coal; and the latter is geologically connected with Germany by a chain of carboniferous rocks. Saxony, Bohemia, and Silesia, have also abundance of coal. In France the dep. of Aveyron is said to be able, if properly developed, to supply all France. At St. Etienne, the heart of the French mining-district, coal can be extracted as low as in Wales; the expense of it throughout France is imputed mainly to the absence of easy lines of carriage and communication, which enable English coal to be sold on the French coasts at a profit; but which again is met by the competition of the coal of Belgium. A prejudice in favour of wood-fuel, and an insufficient national demand, have prevented the French coalfields from developing their great resources. Russia, Syria, the E. Indies, China, Australia, and many other parts of the world, abound with this valuable mineral." In 1581, in consequence of the great consumption of wood which they occasioned, they were restrained by act of parliament. In 1624, a patent for smelting iron with pit-coal was exempted from the law against monopolies; but the use of wood for this purpose seems still to have continued; for in 1719 complaints were made that the waste and destruction of the woods in the cos. of Warwick, Stafford, Worcester, Hereford, Monmouth, Gloucester, and Salop, by the iron-works, was too great to be imagined. At this period about 20,000 tons of iron were annually imported into England from abroad; and subsequently, when our manufactures increased, and before our own mines were properly wrought, the importation was still greater. In 1737, it was computed that England made annually at home about 18,000 tons of bar-iron, which, at the rate of 3 tons of pig-iron for one of bar iron, would give 54,000 tons as the produce of the English mines. In 1785, a method was invented of converting pig-iron into bariron superior to the Swedish bar-iron; by which improvement, it was given in evidence in 1812, when application was made for a parliamentary reward for the invention, that whereas, about 30 years before, the importation of foreign iron averaged about 50,000 tons, in 1810 it was reduced to 20,500 tons; and that our export of iron, which at the former period was only a few cwts., in 1810 amounted to 24,500 tons. In 1814, the iron-mines of England were supposed to supply ore sufficient to produce considerably upwards of 150,000 tons of pig-iron: 10,300 tons of which were made in Derbyshire, and the remainder in Yorkshire, Staffordshire, Shropshire, Lancashire, Monmouthshire, &c. In the last co., there were 9 furnaces, each of which made about 2,000 tons of pig-iron yearly. At Merthyr-Tydvil also, in Glamorganshire, there were furnaces for making pig-iron, which produced 250 tons weekly. In 1848, the number of iron furnaces in England and Wales was 493, and their total produce 1,458,600 tons. See, for further and comparative details, article GREAT

Copper-mines.] Copper-mines were first wrought in England in 1189: in 1561, mines of this metal were discovered in Cumberland; and in 1622 they were wrought in several cos. In 1690, copper was discovered in Wales. But it would appear that during all this period little attention was bestowed on these mines; nor was it before the commencement of the 17th cent. that the copper-inines of Cornwall were properly and effectually wrought. From 1726 to 1735, there was produced 64,800 tons of ore, which afforded the annual quantity of about 700 tons of fine copper; from 1766 to 1775, the amount of ore raised was 264,273 tops, and of copper made 2,650 tons. About the year 1773, new copper mines were discovered in Derbyshire, which circumstance, added to the discovery of the famous copper-mine in Anglesea, forms an important era in the history of this branch of English mining. As long as this latter mine continued productive, it furnished from 5,000 to 10,000 tons per quarter, exclusively of what was obtained from the Mona-mine, which probably was nearly as much; both together employing 1,200 miners, and 90 smelters. But both the Parys and the Mona-mines have for some years produced comparatively little copper-ore. In the meantime the Cornish and Derbyshire ores were wrought with great spirit and success; so that the produce of them, and of the other mines in England and Wales, about the year 1791, had rendered this country one of the principal sources from which the world was supplied with this useful metal, instead of her depending, as formerly, upon foreign mines for a supply of it. Within a few years afterwards, the copper-mines of Devonshire began to be wrought with spirit and success. In 1800 the latter did not produce more than 100 tons of fine copper; in 1804, the produce had increased to 300 tons; in 1805, owing to the great exertions made, in consequence of the high price of copper, the returns of the Cornish and Devon mines reached 7,000 tons of fine copper, valued at £1,260,000. In 1812, according to Mr. Grenfel's Observations on the Coppercoinage, the Cornish and Devon mines yielded 80,000 tons of ore Tin-mines.] In the article CORNWALL will be found a brief sketch of the history of the tin-mines of England. At the commencement of the 18th cent., the mines, which had been neglected during the Civil wars, were again wrought with effect and success, producing, one year with another, rather more than 1,600 tons. From 1720 to 1740, the annual average produce was 2,100 tons; from 1740 to 1750, 2,500; from 1750 to 1760, 2,658; from 2,958; from 1790 to 1800, 3,245 tons. In 1829 their produce was 4,290 tons; and this is supposed to be the average annual produce of these mines at the present date. Lead-mines.] There is undoubted proof that the lead-mines of this country, at least those of Derbyshire, were wrought in the time of the Romans, this metal having been exported A. D. 14; at the time of the Conquest it was used to roof churches. In 1189, that no mines of that metal were known, or at least wrought, except those in Derbyshire. In 1289, lead-mines were discovered and wrought in Wales; and it being ascertained that the ore of these mines produced some silver, increased attention was given to the working of this metal. The produce of the lead-mines at present wrought in England may average 45,000 tons yearly; and it appears that about 25,000 tons of this yield 8 oz. a-ton of silver. In 1845 the total quantity of lead-ore raised in England was 54,853 tons; producing 37,413 tons of lead. The lead-mines of Wales produced 16,412 tons of ore, or 11,011 tons of lead, in 1845. The other metals which are found in England do not require any particular notice, except the black-lead of Cumberland; the manganese of the Mendip-hills, and the zinc of Derbyshire. The first is of excellent quality, and in great demand for pencils, crucibles, &c. No more is wrought than is absolutely necessary to supply the demand, without lowering the price. The manganese of the Mendip-hills is become much more valuable than it formerly was, in consequence of the mode of bleaching by oxymuriatic acid, in which it is largely employed. The quantity of ore of zinc produced in Derbyshire, is about 500 tons annually. It is inferior in value to the calamine of Mendip. Besides calamine, blend or black jack, another ore of zinc, but less valuable than calamine, is got in Derbyshire. Salt-pits.] The salt pits of Cheshire and Worcestershire appear to have been wrought at a very early period: those of the former co. are supposed by Pennant to have been known to the ancient Britons; and the salt-pits in Worcestershire were certainly wrought before the Conquest. In 1389, a license was given for making salt at Middlewich; but nearly 150 years after this, salt appears to have been so rare an article in England, that it was stipulated that a quantity of it should be delivered annually by the king of France to this country. It is rather a singular circumstance, that though the brine-pits were known so early, the pits of rock-salt were not discovered in Cheshire prior to 1670. Salt, up to the year 1823, was burthened with a duty of 15s. per bushel, and the gross quantity made and consumed in England was 296,000 tons. In 1825, with a duty reduced to 2s., the consumption became 342,000 tons. Since that period the article has been free, and the export price is now as low as 34d., while the quantity produced merely to satisfy the foreign demand nearly reached 10,000,000 bushels for only 7 months of 1850. See articles Cheshire, Droitwich, and Worcestershire. Total, Manufactures.] The rise and progress of English commerce and manufactures is sketched in the article British Empire, to which the reader is referred: a few notices of the distribution of the leading manufactures of England and Wales will be appropriately introduced here. Northern counties.] The manufactures of Northumberland are chiefly confined to the town of Newcastle, and the vicinity of the river Tyne. They mostly consist of those coarser manufactures derived from or connected with the coal-trade and mines; such as ship-building, soaperies, glass-works, potteries, ironfounderies, and various other works of a similar kind. Its trade consists chiefly in the export of coals, lead, wrought-iron, grindstones, pottery, glass, &c.—The manufactures of Cumberland are neither numerous nor extensive. They chiefly consist of ginghams, calicoes, corduroys, and other cottongoods, sail-cloth, carpets, paper, pottery, and glassbottles. The harbours of this co. are few, considering the great extent of coast, but its commerce is rapidly improving: the chief ports are Whitehaven, Workington, Maryport, and Harrington.—Manufactures have made little progress in Westmoreland. Its wool is wrought up in the manufactures of Kendal, and of Bradford in Yorkshire; part of it is also wrought into knit-stockings, about Kirkby-Stephen, Orton, and Ravenstonedale.—Manufactories are conducted on a large scale in the co.-palatine of Durham. About 11,000 tons of salt are made yearly from a salt spring near Birsley. At Smallwell and Winlaton are founderies and forges,—the former producing anchors and mooring-chains, the latter files, edgetools, and such hardware as is necessary for artificers. A manufactory for sword-blades has been long established at Shotly. There are several establishments for the fabrication of copperas and salammoniac on the rivers Tyne and Wear. Shipbuilding is carried on to a considerable extent, and the manufacture of cordage. Glass and pottery are made both for home consumption and exportation. at the York and Howden fairs in this district by the London dealers. Hull is its grand emporium of at Wansford near Driffield, for carpets and spinning be pronounced one of the greatest manufacturing at Leeds, Wakefield, Bradford, Halifax, Huddersrow cloths of all qualities, shalloons, calimancoes, and flannels, with every kind of woollen goods. From Sheffield, cutlery and plated goods are exported to all parts of the globe.—The manufactures of Lancashire are very extensive, consisting of cotton, silk, woollen, and linen goods, hats, stockings, pins, needles, nails, watch-tools and movements, snuff, earthenware, porcelain, paper, &c. The cotton-trade especially has thriven here with a rapidity and to an extent beyond all example. Of this vast manufacture, Manchester is the principal seat: thence it spreads on all sides, to the S and E into Cheshire and Yorkshire, but especially to the N and W over the greater part of Lancashire, - extending from Furness to Derby on the one hand, and from Liverpool to Halifax on the other. A variety of other tool-makers, engine and machine-makers, depend for their existence on this manufacture; and there are also in this co. large works for smelting iron and copper, for blowing common and casting plate-glass, and for manufacturing white lead, lamp black, vitriolic acid, and other chemical substances. Counties bordering on Wales.] In various parts of Cheshire, manufactures are carried on extensively in silk, cotton, and linen, ribbons, thread, buttons, and tanning leather. The exports are lead, calamine, cast-iron, copper-plates, brass, salt, and cheese.—The principal manufactures of Shropshire are the ironworks in the coal and iron-district E of Shrewsbury, chiefly at Ketley, Oakengates, and in Colebrookdale, which has also potteries and a coal-tar work. Brosely has a manufacture of garden-pots, and excellent tobacco-pipes. Caughley has a china-manufactory of great excellence; and Coalport furnishes china of all sorts, and also Queen's or Wedgewood's ware. This co. has also several mills for dyeing woollen cloths, and some linen and cotton manufactories.—Ironstone and coal are abundant in Monmouthshire, and numerous extensive iron-works have been erected here. Limestone, breccia for millstones, and valuable building-stone, are abundant in this co. Flannels, coarse cloths, stockings, and knitcaps, are manufactured to a considerable extent. Midland district. Nottinghamshire has been long famed for its manufactures, of which stockings form the staple. Those of thread and British laces have also been long carried on upon an extensive scale. The cotton-trade from Manchester has been widely extended, and is still increasing. There are also in this co. worsted-mills, silk-mills, and manufactories of sailcloth, starch, and coarse red earthenware. Nottinghamshire, being a kind of thoroughfare for the neighbouring cos., has a very extensive and lucrative commerce.—The manufactures of Leicestershire are chiefly and almost entirely those of its great staple, wool; and consist in the combing and spinning of wool, and making it into stockings. The chief exports are wool, worsted stockings, hats, lace, cattle, sheep, and cheese.—A large proportion of the inhabitants of Derbyshire are occupied in trade and manufactures, which are here conducted on a large scale, and consist of iron, woollen, linen, cotton, and silk goods. Cutting and polishing marble and spar, for useful and ornamental purposes, affords a constant source of employment to a numerous class of artisans.—The clays, which are found of almost every variety in Staffordshire, form the basis of one of the most important manufactures in the kingdom, that of earthen ware, for which Staffordshire is celebrated. The potteries are carried on in a district near Newcastle-under-Lyne, occupying an extent of about 10 sq. m., and, though naturally barren, containing a number of populous towns in which the works are carried on. The soil of this co. contains almost every variety of clays, besides cover. of the coast. ing strata of coal, rich and easily worked. From these natural advantages, the pottery business was begun here at least two centuries back; but the improvements of the late Mr. Wedgewood have brought the article to so much perfection that it has become of national importance. Glass is also a flourishing manufacture here; and manufactories of iron and steel of all descriptions are numerous and extensive. Tin and brass are also among its manufactures, with tapes, cotton, and silk to a considerable extent. Tobacco and snuff-boxes, boots, shoes, and hats, are manufactured in Darlaston, Stafford, Newcastle, and Burton, on a large scale. Trade and manufactures have been greatly advanced in this co. by the extensive system of inland navigation, which, long previous to the era of railroads, connected it not only with the metropolis, but with the Severn, the Mersey, the Humber, and the three corresponding ports of Bristol, Liverpool, and Hull.—The manufactures of Northamptonshire are inconsiderable, consisting chiefly in the making of shoes, lace, and woollen stuffs. There is a manufactory of whips at Daventry, and one of considerable extent for making silk-hose. The trade of this co. is pretty extensive, being likewise greatly facilitated by canals.—Warwickshire is conspicuous for commercial enterprise, and for the spirit with which manufactures are cultivated. These last are of various descriptions. Those of hardware at Birmingham are celebrated throughout Europe. In the city of Coventry, and neighbourhood, not less than 16,000 people are employed in the manufacture of ribbons; and for watches, the same place took the lead at one time even of London. Kenilworth is famous for horn-combs; Warwick for hosiery, calicoes, and other cotton-goods; in other places there are flax-manufactures, and much linen-yarn is spun. Alcester employs 900 persons in making needles.— Worcestershire exports gloves, Kidderminster goods, china and glassware, nails, and the smaller articles of iron-work, as well as bar and sheet-iron.—The abundance of coal has drawn extensive iron-works to the Forest of Gloucestershire. Tinplate is made at Fromilade, and edge-tools, brass-wire, wire-cards, pins and nails. The finer wools are manufactured into superfine broad cloth in a district of which the town of Stroud is the centre. The waters of the Stroud are supposed to possess peculiar properties for dyeing scarlet, blue, and black. Thin worsted stuffs and carpets are made at Cirencester, and stockings at Tewkesbury.—The chief manufactures of Buckinghamshire are lace and paper. Most of the poorer class of females are employed in lacemaking, which, with plaiting straw, employs nearly all the working females in the county.—The manufactures of Bedfordshire are confined to the plaiting of straw, and making thread-lace, in which threefourths of the female population are employed. South-eastern district.] Kent has few manufactures, and these mostly of the coarser kind. The clothing-trade, formerly extensive, is now nearly annihilated; and silk, which was long manufactured at Canterbury, has given place to cotton. At Maidstone and Dover are extensive paper-mills; at Deptford and the Isle of Grain, salt-works. There are large copperas-works at Deptford and Whitestaple; and various iron-furnaces in the weald bordering on Sussex. South-western counties.] The manufactures of Somersetshire are considerable, consisting of stockings, woollen cloth, coarse linen, such as dowlas and tick, gloves, &c .- The woollen manufactures of Devonshire, once considerable, have now greatly declined. Roads.] In 1818, according to the Government report on the Turnpike roads of England and Wales, there existed - In England and Wales, paved streets and turnpike-roads to the extent of . 19,725 m. Other public highways, . . . . 95,104 114,829 Total, Parliamentary returns show that in 1829 the length of the turnpikeroads in England and Wales was 20,875 m. In 1839 the length of the turnpike-roads and paved streets throughout England and Wales amounted to 22,534 m., while "all other highways" were 96,991 m. long, making in all 119,527 m. of road. The cos. containing the greatest length of turnpike-roads, according to their size, were Derby, Worcester, Flint, Gloucester, Somerset, Monmouth, Stafford, Hereford, Southampton, &c., which severally contained 1 m. of turnpike-road to about each 1,000 statute acres, -the average for the entire country being nearly double that amount of acres to each mile of road. Those counties, on the other hand, which contained the shortest length of turnpike-roads in relation to their size, were Anglesey, in which there was only 5 m. of road to 173,000 statute acres, being in the proportion of 1 m. to 34,688 acres; then Westmoreland, Suffolk, Essex, Norfolk, Pembroke, and Cumberland. The cos. containing the greatest length of paved streets at the above period were, first, Middlesex, where there was 1 m. of street to every 774 acres; second Suffolk, third Lancaster, fourth Warwick, fifth Surrey, and sixth Chester. The average number of acres to each m. of paved street was 12,734; and in the districts above specified the number of acres to the m. ranged from 3,600 to 6,900. Those cos. which contained the shortest length of streets were Radnor and Anglesey, in which there were no paved streets whatever; Brecon, which has only 1 m.; and Carnarvon, only 2 m.; whereas Middlesex, the co. of the capital, has as many as 232 m. of streets extending through it. The cost of the repairs of the roads and streets in the different cos. varies considerably.
