Telford, M'Adam, and M'Neil, and although our canal-communication likewise saved much time, labour, and expense, yet our heavy taxation, the increasing competition of foreigners, the rapid increase of our large towns, and the value of individual time, in conjunction with the application of steam as a motive power, made the people of England seek some more rapid mode of travelling; and the attention of our civil engineers was directed to the extension of the tram-road system, and the use of steam as a tractive power: the result is the new railway system. Some idea of the different rates of speed in travelling, before and since the institution of railways, may be formed from such facts as the following. Two hundred years ago, King James's groom rode six days in succession between London and York; and a wonderful exertion it was deemed. The time distance now, by railway-express, between London and York, is 6 hours 50 minutes! In 1609, the communication between the north and the universities was kept up by carriers, who pursued their route with whole trains of pack-horses. To their care were consigned the packages and persons of the scholars. It was through their medium that epistolary correspondence was managed; and a letter could scarcely be exchanged between Yorkshire and Oxford in less time than a month. Not earlier than 1638, Mary de Medicis, the queen-mother of France, visiting her daughter, Queen Henrietta, entered London in a litter carried by two mules. Mary, queen of Scots, while under the surveillance of the earl of Shrewsbury, appears to have always travelled on horseback in her various journeys. In 1640, the wife of Henry, last earl of Cumberland, occupied 11 days going to London, and appears, from the state of the roads, to have ridden the whole way on horseback. In December 1703, Charles, king of Spain, slept at Portsmouth, and the prince of Denmark went to meet him. One of his attendants -writes, "We set out at 6 o'clock in the morning, to go to Petworth, and did not get out of the coach-save only when overturned, or stuck fast in the mire-till we arrived at our journey's end. Twas hard service for the prince to sit 14 hours in the coach that day without eating, and passing through the worst ways that I ever saw in my life: our coach would have suffered very often if the nimble boors of Sussex had not frequently poised it, or supported it with their shoulders, from Godalming almost to Petworth." About the end of the reign of George II., 1755 to 1760, the London and Edinburgh coach—the only stage-coach running about that time in Scotland-was advertised to run in 14 days in summer, and 16 in winter; the intervening Sundays on the route being observed by man and beast as days of rest: in other words, the journey between London and Edinburgh then occupied longer time than is now requisite to cross the Atlantic ocean by steam to New York! At that very time, however, the mail, conveyed by boys on horseback between London and Edinburgh, was only 4 days by the way. While by the recently exploded mail-coach system the distance between London and Birmingham occupied 10 hours, it now takes only 4 or 3; and while from London to Bristol the mail was carried in 16 hours, it is now accomplished in 4 and in 21. Referring the reader to the article British Empire for further details on the railroad system of the United kingdom, it may be here stated, that the whole extent of railway communication in England and Wales, at the end of 1849, was 4,656 m.; while that of the whole United kingdom was 5,996 m. Population.] The only official materials for estimating the pop. of England, prior to the returns of the hearth-tax after the Revolution, are the general cadastre of Doomsday-book, in the last years of William the Conqueror; the Subsidy-roll of 51° Edward III., printed in the 7th volume of the Archaeologia; and a muster of the fighting men in the reign of Elizabeth. The first of these—in which, however, the four northern cos., as well as the metropolis, are omitted - gives, according to the synopsis of Sir Henry Ellis, in his recent introduction to Doomsdaybook, a total of 283,242 persons. Few women are included in this enumeration; nor any children, except probably such as were owners of land. Unless the omissions are very considerable, this return does not authorize us to reckon the pop. of England at that period much above 1,000,000. It may be observed, that the severities of William's reign had dispeopled great tracts of country; and that many of the principal towns are shown by this record to have been surprisingly diminished in the number of inhabited houses since the time of Edward the Confessor. The Subsidy-roll of 51° Edward contains a return of all lay persons, male and female, above 14 years of age-real mendicants excepted-all of whom III. * ENGLAND AND WALES. were subjected by a statute of that year (1378) to a poll-tax of a groat. The number stated, exclusive of the cos. of Durham and Chester, and also of Wales, is 1,376,442. It is of importance, in relation to this return, to determine the proportion which must be added for children under 14 years of age. Mr. Chalmers is of opinion that one-half is a liberal allowance; and that the pop. of England and Wales, at the beginning of the reign of Richard II., was about 2,092,978; or, in round numbers, 2,100,000. Compared with Sir Henry Ellis's summary of the returns in Doomsday-book, this would show somewhere about a duplication of the pop. in 300 years. The trustworthiness, however, of this official document, or the validity of the general inference deduced from it, has been disputed in a very ingenious paper by Mr. Amyot published in the 20th volume of the Archaeologia. He finds it difficult to reconcile the numerical pop. of our chief towns, returned in this Subsidy-roll, with what we read in historians of their importance, and what we can ourselves judge of their extent. London, for example, would appear from this document to have contained less than 35,000 inhabitants; Norwich less than 6,000; York, only 10,000. Mr. Amyot does not impeach the correctness of the return for the time wherein it was made; but he ingeniously accounts for a rapid and important decrease of pop. in that age by the great plague of 1349, the most desolating on record in Eubeen computed by several writers at one-half the people, and some popular historians rate it a great deal higher. This defalcation, Mr. Amyot is of opinion, could not have been made up in 28 years, -the interval between 1349 and 1377. In this Mr. Hallam, in a valuable contribution to the papers of the Stapresses an opinion that the elasticity of the principle people after they have been thinned by great calamities, though indisputably true in theory, has been sometimes exaggerated by political economists as to the measure of its force. It is also to be taken into red in the intervening time,—one in 1361, and one in 1369. The third official return of the numbers of the people, Mr. Hallam observes, in the paper referred to, is of 1575, or, as some say, of 1583. The men of England, capable of bearing arms, were then found to be 1,172,000; this number multiplied by 4, according to a common rule which seems tolerably just, gives a total pop. of 4,688,000. If, therefore, the roll of 51° Edward is nearly correct, the pop. of England had more than doubled in 200 years. There seems no reason to suppose that this estimate is above the mark; and the known circumspection of Elizabeth's government leads us to put as much trust in its exactness as statistical returns usually deserve. A document laid before the Statistical society furnishes some data for conjecture as to the numbers of the English people about 1528. It appears that about the 19th of Henry VIII., commissions were issued for the purpose of taking an account of the stock of corn and grain throughout the kingdom; and some fragments of the returns have been preserved in the Record office. The commissioners, it would seem, in making these returns, added an estimate of the number of inhabitants in the districts which they examined, intending apparently to comprehend in this enumeration, all individuals by whom the corn could be consumed, or all individuals excepting infants of the most tender age. In regard to the document itself, the following is a brief summary:- Kent-Hundred The hundreds of which we have here a return, are all agricultural districts, and therefore must have been less affected by peculiar causes of increase of pop. than some parts of the kingdom have been. The comparison with the census of 1831 shows, however, a disproportionate rate of augmentation in these three districts, and Mr. Hallam states that he could not account for all the differences. Taking the three together, we find the pop. of the districts before us, in the reign of Henry VIII., to have amounted to 16,425, while, in 1831, it was 52,392, being an increase of more than 3 to 1. If the pop. of England and Wales, in the middle of the reign of Elizabeth, has been rightly taken at 4,688,000, that entire pop. had only increased in about the same ratio of something more than 3 to 1. But three agricultural districts cannot be fair samples of the general progress of pop. between 1528 and 1831. If the isles of Sheppey and Thanet have tripled their numbers, much more have the cos. of York and Lancaster, besides many others, done so; and, consequently, the average increase of the kingdom must have been in a higher ratio. It has been, however, nearly in this identical ratio, when compared with the returns under Elizabeth; and, therefore, these last returns indicate that the pop. had been in an advancing, and not a stationary or retrograde condiof representing statistical results by diagrams. It might be said, he remarks, that combinations of straight lines are apt to be both unpleasant and perplexing to the eye, while, by adopting curves, we purchase greater neatness, but at an important sacrifice of truth. The course of pop., though varying in a great kingdom perhaps every minute, is capable, in theory, of being represented by a curve line, without impropriety in the eyes of the strictest geometer. We may, therefore, imagine that, at the Conquest-or any other era we may assume-a vertical axis being first drawn, representing the succession of time, a series of ordinates, proportionate to the pop., were drawn at every minute. These ordinates would be bounded by a curve, which would be the symbol of the numerical strength of the kingdom from the Conquest to the present day, if we should continue it so long. This curve would be generally an increasing line, but not always; and would assume on the whole an irregular character. But there is another mode of contemplating this subject. If it be true, as most agree, that there is what may be called the natural tendency of pop., or that which is found in the absence of disturbing circumstances, to increase in a geometrical ratio, there will be a certain curve expressing this possible though never accurately true result; in which, as the abscisses of the axis or vertical line increase in arithmetical series by units of time, the ordinates, which represent the corresponding pop., would increase in a geometrical ratio. Such a line—commonly called 'the logarithmic curve'-will, according to that part of Mr. Malthus's theory which is most generally received, represent the natural law of pop., or that towards which the numerical increments of a people, in the most flourishing and favourable circumstances, would approximate. It is, of course, not meant that the real progression would, under any circumstances of human beings, be delineated by such a curve, ENGLAND AND WALES. increased nearly in the ratio that they would have had to each other, if the curve, which the pop. has actually described, had been mathematically the same as that which the law of natural tendency prescribes. It is evidently not of the slightest importance to the truth of this theory, that the pop. should increase, or have a natural tendency to increase, at any specific rate of acceleration: whether it be in 25 or in 500 years that it doubles itself, the geometrical ratio may be going on uniformly the whole time. It is not, indeed, probable, that exterior circumstances will leave nature so undisturbed for 500 as for 25 years; but, theoretically speaking, there is no difference. The principle of the geometrical ratio has been as remarkably exhibited in Great Britain, during the last 30 years, as it has been during a longer series of terms in the United States of America, though the rate of increase has been less rapid. In consequence of the duplication in about 25 years, which has been experienced for the last cent. in America, it has become usual to assume that number as the legitimate rate of increase which would be found in all countries under equally favourable circumstances. Whether this be altogether true, it requires, perhaps, more extensive researches to decide. The progress of the population of England and Wales, in decennary periods, from the year 1700 to 1841, was as follows: We cannot place entire reliance on any of the above enumerations excepting the five last. An actual enumeration, from survey or census, is the only mode of ascertaining the pop. of a country that can be depended upon; but it was not until 1801 that the sanction of government was obtained for this purpose. The first method which seems to have presented itself to our statisticians for ascertaining the probable amount of the pop. of England, was to multiply the houses by what might be supposed to be the average number of persons occupying a house. Previously to the Revolution, a hearth-duty, or tax proportioned to the number of fire-places in a house, was payable by all houses in the kingdom; and since the Revolution, the number of houses was inferred from the returns made by the collectors of house and window-duties. It is quite clear, however, that neither of these modes could afford very certain data for the calculation in question. The registers of births and burials that are kept in most places, have also been resorted to as a means by which to estimate the magnitude of the pop.; but these registers—previous to the passing of the act for registering births, deaths, and marriages—were seldom either complete or accurate. The earliest of the parish-registers of England date from the establishment of the church of England; injunctions to that effect having been issued by Cromwell, Henry's vicegerent for ecclesiastical jurisdiction, in 1538. The parish-register act of 1812 obviated some of the previous sources of error, and insured the further usefulness of the registers from that period; but a satisfactory system of registration was not established until 1837, when the act for registering births, deaths, and marriages, came into operation in England and Wales. Comparing the returns with those for the ten years ending 1830, the registrar-general inferred that fully 20,000 deaths were omitted annually under the old system. Even in the case of a census, many precautionssuch as it is scarcely possible to get adopted—are requisite to insure perfect accuracy; and there is good reason to think, that, while the last four censuses furnish much nearer approximations to the truth than any other mode formerly devised for ascertaining the amount and classes of the population, they have never yet been perfectly accurate in their results. There can be no doubt, however, that the pop. of England and Wales has been regularly increasing for the last two centuries. The rate of this increase between 1801 and 1811, was 141 per cent.; between 1811 and 1821, 174 per cent.; between 1821 and 1831, 16:01 per cent.; between 1801 and 1831, 16:13 decennially; and between 1831 and 1841, 1.334 per cent. annually. The increase of the 40 English cos. taken together since 1700, up to 1831, was 154 per cent.; and that of the 12 Welsh cos., 117 per cent. But this increase must be attributed in part to the immigration of Scottish and Irish labourers into England. In Cumberland, for example, in 1841, for every 10,000 inhabitants there were 356 persons born in Scotland, and 274 born in Ireland; in Lancashire there were 130 Scotch, and 635 Irish, for the same number of inhabitants: that is to say, one-thirteenth part of the actual pop. of Lancashire was made up of Irish and Scotch in 1841; and nearly one-sixteenth of that of Cumberland. Rate of Mortality.] The increase in the duration of human life in England and Wales is remarkable. From the parish-register returns of the decennial periods of the greater part of the last century, or from 1700 to 1780-which vary from one death in 31 to one death in 42-the average rate of mortality is deduced to have been one death in 37 or 38 of the existing pop. According to the registrar-general's returns, the annual mortality during the seven years 1838-44, was 2.189 per cent., or 1 in 46 of the pop. It was above the average in 1838 and in 1840; near the average in 1839; lower in 1841 and 1842; lowest in 1843 and 1844. It varied from 1 in 43.7 in 1840, to 1 in 47.2 in 1843. In the three years (1838-40), the mortality was 2.239 per cent; in the three (1842-44), 2.147 per cent.,—a fall of one twenty-fourth; but of an equal pop., for 24 deaths in the three first, there were only 23 births in the three last years. The average price of wheat was 67s. 2d. in the three first; and 52s. 10d. in the three last years; the average price of butchers' meat per cwt., paid at Greenwich hospital, was 48s. in the three first, and 44s. 7d. in the three last years. The mean daily wages of bricklayers, masons, plumbers, and carpenters, rose from 5s. 2d. in the one period to 5s. 6d. in the other; the declared annual value of exports from England and Wales was £47,138,173 in the first period, and £48,400,977 in the last; while the annual official value of imports in the corresponding years was £56,269,884, and £62,141,101. The annual amount expended in England on the relief of the poor was £4,581,600 in 1838-40, and £5,074,600 in 1842-44. In the last three years, therefore, the price of provisions was cheaper, commerce and manufactures were more active, relief was more liberally administered to the destitute, and the wages of artisans were higher than in 1838-40. All these circumstances were favourable to the observed reduction of the mortality. The mortality in the division comprising Cheshire and Lancashire rose progressively up to the year 1840, when it was 2.957 per cent., and fell gradually to 2.384 per cent., which it reached in 1844. The mortality of Yorkshire described a similar course. Westmoreland is considerably the most favourable to infant life of any co. in England. Next stand Hampshire, Dorset, Devonshire, and Cornwall; the least favourable is the E. R. of Yorkshire. The rate of mortality in England appears to be lower than in France, Prussia, Austria, and Russia; but neither Scotland nor Ireland is included in the estimate. The diminution of mortality has been most conspicuous in towns. According to the bills of mortality, it appears that the rate of mortality in London, at the beginning of last cent., was less than it afterwards became. It increased from 1700 to 1720, and seems to have attained its maximum in the period from 1720 to 1750. Mr. Rickman states, that the annual mortality in London towards the middle of last cent. was as high as one in 20! But from about 1770 a decided improvement commenced; and in 1800, the mortality had declined to one in 32; in 1820, it did not exceed one in 40. The rate of mortality in Manchester, in 1770, was one in 28; whereas, at present, it is not supposed to exceed one in 45. In 1773, the proportion of deaths to the whole population in Liverpool, was as one to 27.25; but in 1821, the deaths were only in the ratio of one to 44.8. The causes of this remarkable improvement in the duration of human life in England are various: houses less crowded, better clothing, and more cleanliness of habits, cannot have been without some effect; and to these may be added, the increased extent of drainage, which must have acted beneficially on the health of the agricultural pop. The improved treatment of diseases is also another powerful cause. It will be found, cæteris paribus, that the mortality increases as the density of the pop, increases; and where the density and the affluence are the same, that the rate of mortality depends upon the efficiency of the ventilation, and of the means which are employed for the removal of impurities. Domestic progress.] "It would be an interesting investigation," says Dr. Twiss, "to ascertain accurately the corresponding progress of Great Britain, in its various branches of material prosperity. That the prosperity of Great Britain should have increased, would require not merely that its pop. and the means of subsistence should have doubled in a given period—for so long as pop, and subsistence march abreast, no change can take place in the material condition of a people—but that its capital should have increased faster than its pop. A money-estimate of the capital of Great Britain would not enable us to determine this problem; for such an estimate would be so much affected by the variations in the exchangeable value of money as to furnish no safe criterion. For instance, the increased money-value of the rental of the land in England would not necessarily indicate the proportion in which the productiveness of agriculture has been augmented. If in the course of the last 150 years the moneyrents in England should have been trebled, or in some cases quadrupled, we should not therefore be warranted in concluding that the landlord's proportion of the produce had been increased in that ratio. Again, the declared value, or money-value, of our manufactured exports would be a totally false measure of the increase of the quantity exported, if their quantity be looked to as an index of the growth of our manufacturing capital. For our present purpose, therefore, I shall prefer to test the increase in - "Let us take, for instance, the article of grain in the first place. Mr. Charles Smith, in his Tracts on the Corn Trade, estimated the pop. of England and Wales, in 1760, at 6,000,000, - which is sufficiently near the truth for our present inquiry. The entire consumption of grain at that time he estimated to be 7,550,350 qrs., of which 3,750,000 qrs. were wheat, and of the remainder 1,026,125 grs. consisted of barley, 999,000 grs. of rye, and 1,791,225 grs. of oats. The change which has taken place in the species of grain used for bread in England since the period referred to by Mr. ten years, at 29,450,000 qrs., of which 12,450,000 qrs. consist of wheat. It would thus appear that whilst the pop. of England and Wales has doubled, the consumption of wheat, as well as of other grain, has nearly quadrupled; for the home-producer is unable to supply the demand of the consumers, and an annual average of at least 500,000 qrs. of wheat may be added to the total quantity produced at home, on account of foreign importations. In a similar manner, in regard to butchers' meat, if we take the market of the metropolis, we shall find that the number of cattle and sheep annually sold at Smithfield has doubled within the last cent, whilst the weight of the carcass has also more than doubled in that interval. In the early part of last cent. (1710), according to an estimate made by Dr. Davenant, the nett weight of the cattle sold at Smithfield averaged not more than 370 lbs., whilst calves averaged about 50 lbs., and sheep 28 lbs. In 1800 the nett weight of the cattle was estimated at 800 lbs., of the calves at 140 lbs., of the sheep at 80 lbs. Again, in 1742, we find 79,601 head of cattle, and 503,260 sheep, to be the number sold at Smithfield; in 1842 the numbers had increased to 175,347 cattle, 1,438,960 sheep. According to the calculation which Mr. M'Culloch adopted for the amount in 1830, when he sets down 154,434,850 lbs. for the supply of butchers' meat required in London, if we assume the pop. to have then amounted to 1,450,000, exclusively of some suburban districts, we should find the average annual consumption of each individual to be very nearly 107 lbs. In the last edition of his Dictionary, published in 1842, he sets down the consumption, in 1842, at 228,542,340 lbs. for the metropolis, and consequently assigns 120.6 lbs. to each individual, This may, however, be possibly a little above the mark, as the convenience of transport, by railroad, has made Smithfield the central market of a far larger circle than heretofore. The returns obtained by the Statistical society of Manchester, as to the cattle sold in the markets of that town, furnish an annual consumption of not less than 105 lbs. of butchers' meat for each inhabitant. In Paris, on the other hand, the quantity has been estimated by M. Chabrol at from 85 to 86 lbs. per head; and in Brussels it is supposed to average 89 lbs. We thus find that the consumption of animal food, in the towns of England, far exceeds that of foreign cities; and as this consumption has gone on steadily increasing, we are warranted in concluding that the labour of the English people is not only more efficient as compared with that of other nations, but is daily acquiring greater efficiency, if the present be contrasted with previous results. It will be unnecessary to pursue this examination further, as few persons will deny that the absolute increase of the pop, of England and Wales has been accompanied not merely with a proportionate, but a relative augmentation of material prosperity; nor do I think that it can be disputed, that an absolute increase of national prosperity will be invariably attended with an increase of pop. Where the means of subsistence are more easily procured, the natural impulse of man will lead him to marry. Celibacy cannot be a matter of indifference in a healthy state of society, as it is essentially an incomplete state of existence." Poor rates.] The progress of pauperism in England and Wales is noticed in general terms in the article British Empire, p. 55. In 1813, with an estimated pop. of 12,517,921, the poor rates were equivalent to a tax of 10s. 11d. per head upon the whole pop. of England and Wales; in 1834, with a pop. of 14,105,645, they amounted to 11s. 9d. per head; in 1844, estimating the pop. at 16,543,010, to 8s. 3d. per head; and in 1847, to 6s. 1 d. per head, on the estimated pop. 17,325,123. "People do not sufficiently reflect upon the magnitude of numbers, but it may bring the matter a little more clearly to their understanding, if they will consider that the habitual pauperism of England, to say nothing of Ireland, would people several of our largest towns and cities; that, in fact, it would fill Manchester and Salford, Liverpool, Birmingham, Sheffield, Leeds, Edinburgh, and Glasgow, or the entire of London and its suburbs. If these cities and towns were entirely occupied by paupers living idly and unproductively upon the industry and the resources of the rest of the country, the evil would be considered too gross to be tolerated. An article in the Law Magazine, thus sums up the criminalizing influences at work in England and Wales: "In the mining district it appears that there is the least, and in the metropolitan the most crime. In fact, the crime in the latter is more than four times over that of the former district. The districts stand thus, in respect to criminality:—Below the average for England in crime,—Mining, silk, agricultural. Above the average in crime, -Cotton, iron, metropolitan. We see that the silk-district, next to the mining, is the freest from crime; the agricultural being much less free from it than they are. This will surprise many who hold rural pursuits and habits alone favourable to the innocence and morality of the poor. It has also been usual to assign to Lancashire and the great hives of our cotton-industry the worst effects on the mind and morals; this is clearly erroneous, -the iron districts far outstrip them. Again, the metropolisthe focus of all eminence in civilization and intellect—would not be readily deemed the lowest in point of public morals, and yet that is the fact. There is a criminalizing effect in large and crowded communities,-perhaps much owing to the old truth, that deeds of darkness shun the light; and crimes have freest scope where they have most shelter. Again, human habits and natures spread by contact, much as diseases by contagion; but human nature is prone to evil, and, where left to its own dominion, will naturally engender evil: thus vice grows most in crowds, where Christian energies are weak. The mining-districts of Northumberland, Cumberland, Durham, and Cornwall contain fewer large towns than any of the other districts. They are peopled by a race who are of more primitive and simple habits, and they are much more alive to the claims of neighbourhood and the sympathies of Christians than those in any of the great hives of town life. We attach much weight to these influences—to class of industry but little, though its dangers are perhaps also productive of restraint. Peril to life is a quickener to the moral sense, and tends slightly, yet surely, to the virtues which prepare for the future state it keeps in mind. To no class of men is the barrier between life and death slighter than among pitmen. If lightning killed a hundred thousand of the population every year, then a thunder-storm would realize to us the uncertainty of life and suddenness of death that reigns in mining districts. Thus an awe, partly religious, and greatly superstitious, obtains among the people, and checks vice. Children in these districts are less lawless, and more subordinate to parental control; thus a moral restraint exists over the young, which has no existence whatever in the iron and factory districts, where the children are their own masters, and earning an independent livelihood at twelve years of age. There is another element in the case, which also tends to the greater moral restraint in the mining districts-namely, that, of the whole number of offenders, females are a far smaller proportion than in any other district, especially as regards the vast vice of theft, indicative of demoralization more than any other. This prevails also among the men. The comparative scarcity of thefts in the mining districts remarkably confirms the noted character of the population for honesty." This difference is ascribed, amongst other causes, to an actual increase in the proportion of illegitimate children during the operation of that important change in the poor-laws which threw the charge of maintaining their illegitimate offspring upon the mothers. Representative system.] The precise period when counties, cities, and boroughs, were first regularly represented in parliament in England, is a point involved in considerable doubt. The descriptions by our ancient historians of the constituent members of the great national councils are vague and indefinite, and they often state that resolutions were proposed or adopted with the consent of classes of men whose presence they had not previously noticed. Unquestionably, the privilege of attendance was extended to all the tenants-in-chief of the Crown by knight-service; but there are many historical passages which show that-though in point of constitutional right not essential—there were often others present, not as spectators merely, but assisting in the deliberations. The most decisive examples of the gradual rise of a settled plan of representation are to be found after the date of John's great charter. Immediately before that event, however, and in the 15th year of John's reign, writs were issued to the sheriffs of different English cos., directing them to require the presence of four knights out of their respective cos. at a council to be holden at Oxford; and it appears from the Annals of Burton-sub. ann. 1255-that the whole body of the clergy sat by representatives in the 39th of Henry III. The 12th century is the first epoch of civic enfranchisement. London got its charter from Henry I. on the day of his accession, in 1100, and had the priority of all the other cities of England. But even after the rise of corporate borough's in England, the aids of the burgesses were certainly not imposed by the parliament or great council. In the writ issued in the 49th of Henry III., enjoining the attendance of representatives of boroughs, only two-the cities of York and Lincoln—are particularly mentioned: a separate writ appears to have been sent to London and the cinque ports, and then there is a general direction, 'ceteris burgis Angliæ,' to attend by two of their burgesses. In Leicester's parliament of 1265, writs were issued in the king's name to various cities and boroughs, as well as to the cos., each of which was commanded to return 2 representatives to parliament, and the cinque ports 4. This parliament consisted of 166 members, who, it is to be inferred, were elected by some extensive franchise. This innovation—if it was one—was not lost sight of; and though in some parliaments in the beginning of the reign of Edward I., representatives of boroughs may not have been summoned, we have unquestionable evidence of their occasional presence at least, even during that period; and after the 21st year of the first Edward's reign, they formed an undoubted part of the parliament of the kingdom. —Prynne holds that the original right of voting was in the inhabitants at large, without reference to the possession of any landed property whatever. Be this as it may, the statutes 8° and 10° Henry VI., and subsequent acts, restricted the right to persons having a freehold estate in the co. for which they vote of the clear yearly value of 40 shillings; and this continued to be the necessary qualification until the passing of the Reform act. The word freehold being construed to refer to the tenure as well as the quantity of estate, copyholders were excluded from the privilege of voting. By the Reform act, the elective franchise in cos. is now extended to leaseholders, copyholders, and tenants paying a certain rental, in addition to the old 40s. freeholders.—The voters for cities, towns, and boroughs, under the old franchise, were of various descriptions in different places. There were 1st, voters of burgage-tenure; 2d, corporate freemen; 3d, freeholders; 4th, inhabitants; 5th, leaseholders and copyholders. As the rights of these different classes of voters are preserved to them under the Reform act, with certain restrictions, it may be generally freehold for life, or in fee, yet both leaseholders and copyholders paying a burgage rent have been admitted to vote. As to corporate freemen, or free burgesses, their right of election is comparatively modern, and is defined by charters which are still in existence. The right of voting in corporate towns is sometimes in the whole corporation, comprising an indefinite number of freemen; in some cases it is and common council, or the mayor and capital burgesses. In some corporate towns, the elective franchise is vested in freemen of all descriptions, viz., riage with a freeman's daughter, by purchase or redemption, and even in honorary freemen. Another species of voters in cities and boroughs are freeholders, who are not restricted as to the value of their estate as in cos. The right of voting by inhabitants, in the full and most unrestricted meaning of the word, existed nowhere except in Preston; but was generally restricted by the addition of some qualification, as,-paying scot and lot,-being a householder, or potwaller,—or being legally settled. The terms scot and lot are construed to mean parish-taxes or parish-payments. Potwallers are such persons as furnish their own diet, whether householders or lodgers. Leaseholders for terms of not less than three years, and common copyholders having occupied for 40 days before the election, were admitted to vote in the borough of Cricklade alone. In cities and towns which are counties of themselves, the voters are similar to those in other cities and towns, except that in some of the former 40s. freeholders have had the privilege of voting. The Reform bill introduced a new and extensive body of electors into cities and boroughs, viz., occupiers of houses of the annual value of £10 and upwards. The number of such houses in the cities and boroughs of England and Wales returning members, was, in 1846, 498,425, of which 10,665 were in Wales. These will, it is probable, ere long entirely displace the anomalous genera and species of voters above enumerated. At present there are no fewer than 85 different kinds of franchise; and it is easy to conceive that numerous obstructions to its exercise must arise out of such a complicated system. In 1849 somewhat less than one in every 19 persons had a vote in the English cos., and one in 18 in the Welsh cos. In the boroughs there was, in 1849, one voter for every 17 persons in England, and for every 21 in Wales. In the 13th cent. the average qualification of a knight of the shire varied from £20 to £40 yearly value in land. The pay of a knight was usually from 3s. to 5s. per day; that of a citizen or burgess from 2s. to 3s.; sums respectively equivalent to much larger sums at the present time. Parishes.] Parishes were originally considered as ecclesiastical divisions, but are now regarded as civil divisions also. The name comes from the Latin parochia. England was very early divided into parishes, and the boundaries of them, as marked in Dooms. day-book, agree very nearly with the present division. They were recognised by the laws of King Edgar, about the year 970, who directs that the tithes of lands should be paid to the church of the parish in which they were situated. It seems pretty clear and certain, says Blackstone, that the boundaries of parishes were first ascertained by those of a manor or manors; because it very seldom happens that a manor extends itself over more than one parish, though there are often many manors in one parish.- Camden reckoned 9,234 parishes in England; and Chamberlayne 9,913. The total number of parishes—ecclesiastically speaking— has materially increased during the last twenty years; and for any general purpose the number of parishes and parochial chapelries in England and Wales may safely be taken at 12,000.—Some extensive parishes in the northern cos. were divided, in the 13° Charles II., into townships, for the more effectual administration of the poors' laws,—each township maintaining its own poor. Ecclesiastical arrangements.] For ecclesiastical purposes England is divided into two provinces: viz., Canterbury and York, which have the following suffragan bishoprics appertaining to them respectively: 1st. To the province of Canterbury belong Canterbury, London, Winchester, Litchfield, Lincoln, Ely, Salisbury, Exeter, Bath and Wells, Chichester, Norwich, Worcester, Hereford, Rochester, Oxford, Peterborough, Gloucester and Bristol, Llandaff, St. David's, St. Asaph, and Bangor. 2d. To the province of York belong York, Durham, Carlisle, Chester, Ripon, and Man. All the English bishops are peers of the realm, except the Benefices. bishop of Man; and as such, they sit and vote in the house of 11 lords. They are barons, have precedence of all other barons, and rank next to viscounts. The archbishop of Canterbury is the first peer in Great Britain next to the royal family; and the archbishop of York is the third; the lord high-chancellor taking rank betwixt them. The archbishop of Canterbury writes himself, 1,629 of "By Divine Providence, Primate of all England;" the archbishop of York, "By Divine Permission, Primate of England." The bishops of London, Durham, and Winchester, have precedence of all other bishops; the others rank according to seniority of consecration. - Besides archbishops and bishops, there are deans and prebendaries, who assist the bishop in the duties of his * office; and archdeacons, who induct into benefices, reform abuses of smaller importance, and inspect the property of the church. The number of deans in 1850 was 29; of archdeacons, 58. The inferior clergy consist of rectors or priests, where the tithes are entire; vicars, where the tithes are in secular hands; and curates, who perform the ordinary duties of a priest in the place of one who has several benefices. The lowest order of clergy are deacons, who are empowered only to perform the ceremony of baptism, to read in the church, and, at the communion, hand the cup to the priest. To be eligible as a deacon, the candidate must be 23 years of age; a priest must be 24; and a bishop 30. The church-wardens superintend the repairs of the church. Ecclesiastical revenues.] The ecclesiastical commissioners reported, in 1835, that the total amount of the gross annual revenues of the several archiepiscopal and episcopal sees in England and Wales was £181,631, affording an average of £6,727; and the total amount of the nett annual revenues of the same was £160,292, affording an average of £5,936. The total amount of the gross annual revenues of the several cathedral and collegiate churches in England and Wales, in 1835, was £284,241, and the nett annual revenues of the same was £208,289. The total amount of the gross annual separate revenues of the several dignitaries and other spiritual persons, members of the cathedral and collegiate churches in England and Wales, in 1835, was £75,854; and the nett annual separate revenues of the same was £66,465. The total number of benefices, with and without cure of souls, the incumbents whereof made returns to the commissioners' inquiries in 1835, omitting those which are permanently or accustomably annexed to superior preferments, was 10,540; the number of incumbents in 1850 was 10.784. The total amount of the gross annual revenues of the benefices in 1835 was £3,197,225, giving an average income of £303; and the nett annual revenues of the same was £3,004,721, giving an average income of £285. The total number of benefices, with and without cure of souls, in England and Wales, including those not returned, but exclusive of those annexed to other preferments (about 24 in number), was 10,718, the total gross income of which, calculated upon the average of those returned, would be £3,251,159, and the nett income thereof £3,055,451. The number of curates in 1850 was 4,837.—The number of Dissenting, Wesleyan, and Roman Catholic chapels in England and Wales is about 12,000. In 1830, the number of Romish chapels and missions in England and Wales was 382; in 1850, they were returned at 583. English language.] The English language is derived from several sources. The chief part of it is Gothic, introduced by the Saxons. Next to the words of Gothic origin, are those derived from the Latin, and chiefly through the medium of the French. The Saxon part of the language was introduced by the Saxons at the conquest. The French part was in a great measure introduced by William I. A number of terms have been directly introduced from the Latin, Greek, and other languages. The consequence of all this blending and interfusing of different dialects has been that we now possess a copious language, easily applicable to every subject, and exhibiting a greater degree of ductility than any of the languages of Europe, with the exception perhaps of the German. Dr. Johnson's dictionary-which is still the standard dictionary of the language-contains the following words, but later lexicographers profess to enumerate from 5,000 to 10,000 words more:- 3 | Participial nouns, Articles, Total, The earliest specimen we have of English is the song of the elder Cædmon, 'On the Origin of Things,' inserted by King Alfred in his translation of Bede. As Cædmon died in 680, this specimen is about 1,200 years old:- Nu we sceolan herigean Now we should praise Heafon rices weard; Heaven-kingdom's warder; Metodes mihte, Creator mighty, And his mod gethane, And his mind thought, Glorious Father of his works! Weorc wulder fæder! Swa he wuldres gehwæs As he, of glory every, Eternal Lord! Ece drihten! Tha midden geard, The mid region, Mon-cynnes weard, Mankind's warder (guardian), Ece drihten, The eternal Lord, Æfter teode Afterwards made Firum foldan; The ground for men, Frea Almihtig. Ruler Almighty! Welsh language.] The Welsh language is radically independent of both European and oriental dialects, though several modern languages are intermixed with it. Nennius, who wrote in the 9th cent., mentions the bards Talhaian, Tatangun, Nuevin, Bluchbar, and Cian, as renowned for their skill in poetry; and Aneurim, Taliesin, and Llywarch the aged, as professors of the same art, so early as the 6th cent. The oldest indisputably genuine work in Welsh is Howel Dha's Laws, a work of the 10th cent. An English student of the Welsh tongue is certainly liable to be perplexed by the application of letters, with which he is familiar, to the expression of sounds very different from those which he has been accustomed to assign to them: still it is to be regretted that so little attention has been paid by British scholars to the acquisition of the ancient British dialects. The number of scholars who have some knowledge ENG of Latin, Greek, or French, in proportion to those who know anything of the Welsh language, is probably at least as 500 to one. Public journals.] In 1826, the number of newspapers stamped for circulation in England and Wales was 25,681,003; in 1849, the number of 1d. stamps for newspapers in England and Wales was 66,159,502; and of \(\frac{1}{2}\)d. stamps, 10,309,233. Islands. The most remarkable islands connected with England, are those of Wight, Guernsey, Jer- SEY, ALDERNEY, SCILLY, ANGLESEA, and MAN. See these articles.

ENGLEBERG, a parish and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Unterwalden, 9 m. SE of Sarnen, on the Stanz-Aa, at an alt. of 3,280 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,900. There is a fine Benedictine abbey here, which was founded in 1120. It has a large church, and a library of 10,000 vols.—To the S of the abbey rises the Tiblisberg, to an alt. of 7,530 ft. above the valley, and 11,414 ft. above sea-level.
ENGLEFIELD, a parish of Berks, 4 m. W of Reading. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 373.
ENGLEFIELD BAY, an indentation of the W coast of Queen Charlotte's island, in North America, in N lat. 53°, W long. 132° 30'.
ENGLEFIELD (CAPE), a headland of Melville peninsula, at the W entrance of Hecla and Fury strait, in N lat. 69° 50', W long. 85° 30'.
ENGLE-FONTAINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. and 21 m. from Le Quesnoy. Pop. 1,572. It has a porcelain manufactory.
ENGLESQUEVILLE, or ANGLESQUEVILLE, a town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, the St. Lawrence, in N lat. 49° 15', W long. 68'.
ENGLISH BAZAAR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, district and 60 m. SW of Dinajpur, near the r. bank of the Mahanada, 30 m. NNW of its confluence with the Ganges.
ENGLISH (CAPE), a headland of the S coast of Newfoundland, on the E side of St. Mary's bay, in N lat. 46° 50′, W long. 53° 40′.

ENGLISH CAY, an island off the coast of Honduras, on the S side of the entrance of the Belize channel. ENGLISH CHANNEL,—the La Manche of the French,—that narrow sea which separates England on the S from the N shores of France. On the W it opens into the Atlantic by a wide mouth, the extreme points of which are the Land's End in Cornwall, and the island of Ushant on the French coast. The distance between these points is about 100 m. On the E the E. channel opens into the North sea by the straits of Dover,-the Pas-de-Calais of the French. This strait, which is the narrowest part of the channel, is formed on the English side by the coast stretching between the S. Foreland and Dungeness, and on the French side by that extending from the harbour of Calais to Boulogne; and at its narrowest point, between the S. Foreland and Cape Grisnez, is only about 18 m, across in a SE direction; at several other points it is very little more. The distance between Dungeness and Cape Grisnez is 23 m. SE by E; and from the S. Foreland to Calais, 201 m. SE by S. To the W of the strait the channel rapidly increases in width. Thus, between Beachy-head and Cape L'Ailly it is 57 m. S & E; between Brighton and Havre-road it is 80 m. S by W; between St. Catherine's-point on the isle of Wight, and Cape Barfleur, the termination of the French peninsula of Cotentin, the distance is 53 m. SSW. The widest part of the channel, between St. Alban'shead in Dorsetshire, and the harbour of St. Malo, is nearly 140 m. across. The current generally, if not constantly, runs in the E. channel from W to E. In stormy weather the surface of the channel is raised 2 ft. or more above that of the North sea, and the ports of the channel have some feet more of water in strong W winds than at ordinary times. Generally speaking, the water in the entrance of the channel is from 8 to 10 fath. deeper toward the French coast than on the English; yet the French ports along the channel are shallow, and none of them are naturally deep enough to admit men-of-war, while England has some of its finest harbours on the channel.—The E. channel abounds with fish; and the fishings give occupation to a considerable number of fishermen on the coasts of England and France. The most important branch of the Channel fisheries is the pilchardfishery along the coast of Cornwall and Devonshire. Before the convention between the English and French governments, the French fishermen were accustomed to fish with large fleets of boats, frequently within half-a-league of the English shore; while the English fishermen were not permitted to approach so close to the French shore. The French vessels were also larger and better manned than the English; carried nets and fishinggear of greater power; and continued on our coasts during the whole course of the herring and mackarel seasons. In 1839 a convention was agreed upon between the English and French governments, for the regulation of the fisheries in the channel. The distance of 3 m. beyond low-water mark on either side-except Granville bay-on the French coast, in N lat. 48° 50', is reserved expressly for the fishermen of that coast, and 3 m. from the headlands of all small bays. Granville bay is subjected to special provisions. All the portion of the channel which is more than 3 m. distant from low-water mark on either coast is common to the fishermen of both countries. All the fishing boats of both nations are to be numbered and lettered on the bow and on the mizen sail, in such a way as to indicate the fishing-district to which they belong. The buoys, barrels, net-floats, and other fishing apparatus, are to be numbered in correspondence with the boats. Trawl-fishing may be carried on during all seasons, in the sea lying between the fishery-limits, with trawls of which the meshes of the net are not to be less than 13 inch from knot to knot, but must not be carried on within 3 m. of any spot where boats happen to be engaged in herring or mackarel driftnet fishing. Herring-fishery is free all the year round, but the meshes of the net are not to be below a certain proscribed size. Mackarel-fishing is placed under similar regulations. Oysterfishing is to open on September I, and to close on April 30. The oyster-dredging is not to be carried on between sunset and sunrise; all oysters below a certain size are to be thrown again into the sea. No boat may anchor between sunset and sunrise on grounds where herring or mackarel drift-net fishing is going on. Fishing cruisers belonging to each nation are to enforce the observance of the regulations; their commanders have powers of arbitration in trivial sources of dispute, but for graver matters they take possession of the fishing boats, and deliver up the men to the proper authorities.
ENGLISH-COMBE, a parish of Somersetshire, 2 m. SE of Bath. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 486.

ENGLISH COMPANY'S ISLANDS, a group at the entrance of Arnheim bay, on the coast of N. Australia, to the E of Wessel islands. The largest of the group, situated in S lat. 12°, E long. 136° 15', is 15 m. in length, and about 6 m. in breadth.

ENGLISH COVE, a harbour on the SW coast of the island of New Ireland or Tombara, in the S. Pacific, in S lat. 4° 54', E long. 152° 30'. It was discovered by Cartaret in 1767. It abounds in fish, especially shell-fish of large size; and its banks are covered with cocoa-nut and other trees.

ENGLISH HARBOUR, an indentation of the S coast of the island of Antigua, in the p. of St. Paul, in N lat. 17° 3′ 30", W long. 61° 45'. This harbour is one of the best in the West Indies. It has a narrow entrance, but its interior is safe, spacious, and well-sheltered; and it has depth sufficient for vessels of the largest size. It has an extensive dock-yard and a marine arsenal; and situated on the hills which form the embrasure are the forts, barracks, and an hospital.—Also a bay on the W coast of Costa Rica, at the mouth of the Hugueren, in N lat. 8° 50', W long. 83° 55'.—Also a town on the NW coast of the island of Bonavista, in the Cape Verd group.
ENGLISH ISLAND, an island in the gulf of Smyrna, in N lat. 38° 30', E long. 26° 44'.

ENGLISH NARROWS, an interior sound in Concepcion strait, in Patagonia. It is 12 m. long, and from a <math>\frac{1}{4}</math> m. to <math>1\frac{1}{4}</math> m. wide; but is in many places contracted by islands to a width not exceeding 400 yds. Its S end is in S lat. 49° 06', W long. 74° 13' 20".
ENGLISH NEIGHBOURHOOD, a village of Hackensack township, Bergen co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 8 m. N of New York. Pop. in 1840, about 100.
ENGLISH REACH, a channel of South America, in Magalhaen's straits, between Capes Gallant and Holland. Its greatest breadth is 10 m. At its S extremity are Wren, Charles, and Carlos isles.

ENGLISH RIVER, a river of Lower Canada, in the seignory of Beauharnais, an affluent of the Cha- ENH teauguay.—Also an extensive estuary on the SE coast of Africa, opening into Delagoa bay, between Point Reuben on the N, and Point Mawhone on the S; in S lat. 25° 59', E long. 32° 32'. It is the common estuary of the Mattoll, the Dundas or Chienca, and the Temby, Elephantine, or Chirendene rivers. Its depth varies from 1 to 7 fath.; its breadth from 1 to 2 m. Its banks are flat, and densely covered with mangroves. At its mouth, on Point Reuben, is the Portuguese fort of São-Lourenço. See Delagoa.
ENGLISHMAN'S BAY, an indentation of the NW coast of the island of Tobago, in the p. of St. John's, in N lat. 11° 19', W long. 60° 43'. It forms a good and commodious harbour.
ENGLISHTOWN, a village of Freehold township, Monmouth co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 31 m. E of Trenton. It consisted in 1840 of about 30 dwellings.

EN-GOYO, or Angoy, a kingdom of Lower Guinea, bounded on the S by the Zaire; and on the W by the Atlantic. It is supposed to be about 135 m. in length from E to W, and about 30 m. in breadth, and is to a great extent covered with forests. The temperature during the day is very high, but the nights are generally damp and chilly. The principal productions of the soil are maize, tobacco, legumes, sugar-cane, and cotton: the two latter grow indigenously. The natives make a fermented liquor from a species of palm which is found here. Salt forms the chief article of inland commerce.—The capital, Kabenda, is situated on the coast, about 50 m. N of the mouth of the Zaire.
ENGRACE (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Fardets. Pop. 1,457.
ENGSTINGEN (GROSS and KLEIN), two adjacent villages of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Schwarzwald, bail. and 9 m. SSE of Reutlingen, containing respectively 647 and 495 inhabitants.
ENGTER, a parish of Hanover, in the principality and 8 m. N of Osnabruck. Pop. 694. It has a soap manufactory.
ENGUA, a town of Katunga, in W. Africa, 25 m. NNE of Jadu. Captain Pearce died here.

ENGU'A-GUAÇU', an island of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Paulo, formed by an arm of the sea, and connected by a causeway 3 m. in length with the mainland. It is about 18 m. in circumf., and is low, marshy, and consequently unhealthy. It is divided into two districts, São-Vincente, containing a town of the same name, and Barra-Grande.

ENGUERA, a town of Spain, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 39 m. SSW of Valencia, in a mountainous locality. Pop. 5,751. It has manufactories of cloth and other woollen fabrics, and has a considerable trade in cattle.—The partido comprises 12 towns.
ENGUIAS, a river of NW. Africa, in the district of Sahel, which flows into the Atlantic 35 m. NNE of Cape Bojador.
ENGUIDANOS, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 48 m. SE of Cuenca, partido of Requena, on the r. bank of the Cabriel.

ENGUINEGATTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Fauquemberg, 13 m. S of St. Omer. Pop. 432.—The battle of Spurs, in which the French were defeated by the English in 1513, took place in this locality.
ENGUSAH, a town of Barbary, in the district of Bled-el-Jerrid, 15 m. E of Ghargala, on the N side of the Jebel-Wegiad.
ENGWEIRUM, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Friesland, 5 m. ESE of Dokkum, near the shore of the Lauwer-Zee.
ENHALLOW, an island in the group of the Orkney islands, in the p. of Ronsay and Egilshay. It is about 1 m. in circumf., and had 26 inhabitants in 1841.
ENHAM-KNIGHTS, a parish in Hmpshire, 2 m. N of Andover. Area 2,490 acres. Poap. 92.

ENIMIE (SAINTE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lozère, arrond. of Florac. The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 4,169; in 1841, 4,124.—The town is 12 m. WNW of Florac, on the Tarn, by which it is divided into two unequal parts. Pop. 1,219. It has manufactories of serge. It derives its origin and name from a Benedictine priory.

ENIOUSSES, an Esquimaux tribe inhabiting the shores of the Arctic ocean, in British N. America. They are described by Captain Parry as extremely unprepossessing in appearance. They seem to avoid intercourse with the other Esquimaux tribes.

ENKHUIZEN, or ENKHUYSEN, a fortified town of Holland, in the prov. of N. Holland, cant. and 28 m. NNE of Amsterdam, on a peninsula of the Zuyder-Zee. Pop. 5,280. It is well-built, and contains a handsome town-house with a lofty tower, several churches, and an hospital; and has a large cannonfoundry, several salt-refineries, and building-docks. The port, formerly a good one, has been rendered nearly useless by accumulations of sand; but its trade in fish, salt, timber, cattle, cheese, and butter, is still considerable. E. returns a deputy to the States.—This town was formerly the residence of the kings of Fries, and is noted as the place where Charles II. assembled his fleet for his expedition to Spain.

ENKIRCH, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency and 35 m. SW of Coblenz, and 5 m. SW of Zell, on the r. bank of the Moselle, at the confluence of the Grossbach. Pop. 1,971. In the environs are a quarry of slate, and the remains of an ancient town.

ENKOPING, or Enkioping, a small town of Sweden, in the laen and 30 m. SW of Upsala, on an affluent of Lake Mälar. Pop. 1,253. It has a port, the trade of which consists chiefly in the export of the metals of the locality, and of the produce of the surrounding market-gardens.
ENMORE, a parish of Somerset, 4 m. WSW of Bridgewater. Area 930 acres. Pop. 302.
ENNEDA, a well-built town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 11 m. ESE of Glarus, on the r. bank of the Linth, which is here crossed by a bridge. Pop. 2,129.

ENNEL, or Belvedere Lough, a sheet of water in co. Westmeath, 2 m. S of Mullingar, 4 m. in length, and about 2 m. in breadth, and comprising a total area of 3,603 acres. Alt. above sea-level in winter, 274 ft.; in summer, 278 ft.
ENNERDALE, a chapelry in the p. of St. Bees, Cumberland. Pop. 183.

ENNERDALE-WATER, a lake in Cumberland, 1 m. E of Ennerdale, and about 7 m. NE of Egremont. It receives the Eken, and is <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> m. in length, and about <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> m. in breadth.
ENNERICH, a village of Nassau, in the bail. and 1½ m. W of Runkel, on the r. bank of the Worsbach. Pop. 192. It has a paper mill.
ENNERINGEN, a village in the principality of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen, NE of Sigmaringen.
ENNETBUHL, or ENATBUHL, a parish and village in Switzerland, cant. of Gallen, bail. of Obertoggenburg. Pop. 650.
ENNETIERES-EN-WEPPES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Haubourdin. Pop. 1,789.
ENNEVELIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Pont-à-Marcq. Pop. 1,684.

ENNEZAT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of ENN Riom. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,714; in 1841, 9,848.—The town is 7 m. E of Riom, on the l. bank of the Enbenne. Pop. 1,513.

ENNIS, a borough, and the cap. of co. Clare, situated in the p. of Dromcliffe, on the river Fergus, immediately below the influx of the Clareen rivulet, 18 m. NW by W of Limerick, and 1113 m. WSW of Dublin. The streets are in general narrow, crooked, and irregularly edificed. The public buildings consist of the abbey ruins, the parish-church, a large Roman Catholic chapel, a Methodist meeting-house, the court-house, the county-gaol, the county-infirmary, the fever hospital, the lying-in-hospital, the workhouse, the market-house, the town-hall, the linen-market, three bridges across the Fergus, one bridge across the Clareen, some schools, and one or two other structures of little moment; but, excepting the old abbey, they present no architectural features which deserve special notice. E. seems to have prospered more in the last cent. than the present; for the statist of the co. says respecting it in 1808, "It is estimated by the best-informed of the inhabitants to contain about 9,000 souls; 20 years ago it was much more;" while in 1831 it was 1,289 less; in 1841 it was 9,318. A considerable amount of agricultural produce is weekly purchased here, and forwarded for shipment to Clare; and a little is done in the linen and flannel trade. Connected with the town are a brewery, large flour-mills, and a limestone quarry. The borough sends 1 member to parliament. Constituency in 1841, 230; in 1849, 114.
ENNISCOFFEY, or Inniskey, a parish in co. Westmeath, 4 m. SSE of Mullingar. Area 4.466 acres. Pop. in 1831, 939; in 1841, 872.
ENNISCORTHY, or St. Mary's of Enniscor-
THY, a parish in co. Wexford, containing the chief part of the town of E. Area 2,990 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,938; in 1841, 5,538.

ENNISCORTHY, a market and corporate town, chiefly in the p. of St. Mary's of Enniscorthy, and partly in the p. of Templeshannon, co. Wexford, on the river Slaney, 53 m. SSW of Ferns, and 62 m. S by W of Dublin. The chief part of the town is situated on the side of a steep rising ground; its E section is immediately overhung by the notorious Vinegar-hill; and its environs along the river are richly wooded. Its streets in general are nearly as remarkable for their narrowness as for their irregularity and capricious crookedness; and are for the most part both edificed and agglomerated in such a fashion as to give the town a very ancient appearance. The bridge which spans the Slaney, and connects the two parts of the town, is a handsome stone structure of 3 arches. The castle of E., situated on the banks of the river, opposite the remains of the Franciscan friary, is one of the noblest as well as earliest military structures of the Anglo-Norman settlers. It is a massive square pile, flanked at each corner with a round tower, and retaining, in spite of frequent alterations, very distinct traces of a large proportion of its original interior arrangement. The Slanev is navigable to the town for sloops, and constitutes the grand facility for its trade, by far the greater part of the corn sold in the markets being sent down the river to Wexford. Pop. of the whole in 1831, 5,955; in 1841, 7,016. Pop. of the St. Mary's section in 1831, 4,375; in 1841, 5,119.
ENNISCRONE, a fishing-village in co. Sligo, on the E shore of Killala bay, 5 m. SSW of Easkey.
ENNISKEEN, a parish in co. Cavan, but partly in co. Meath. Its Cavan section contains the town of Kingscourt. Area 18,705 acres. Pop. in 1831, 10,368; in 1841, 11,548.

ENNISKEEN, or Inniskeen, a parish chiefly in co. Monaghan, but partly also in co. Leinster. It is * situated on the river Fane, and on the road from Dundalk to Magheross, 33 m. N of Louth. Area of the Monaghan section, 4,990 acres; of the Leinster section, 1,203 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,698; in 1841, 3,732.—Also a village in the p. of Kinneigh, co. Cork, 7 m. W by S of Bandon. Pop. in 1841, 417.

ENNISKERRY, a village in the p. of Powerscourt, co. Wicklow, on the road from Dublin to Glendalough, 21 m. W by S of Bray. Cottages, after the old English style, and in fine keeping as to character and disposition with the surrounding scenery, erected by Lord Powerscourt, and scattered in an irregular and picturesque manner over a declivity, compose the great body of the village. The v., which has become a favourite resort of the citizens of Dublin, stands in the near vicinity of the Scalp, the Waterfall, the Dargle, Powerscourt, Tinnehinch, and other localities famous for their picturesque beauty.—Also a cluster of islets in co. Clare, a little N of the centre of Malbay, and from 13 m. to 23 m. NNW of Dunbeg-bay. The largest one, and indeed the only one of any consequence, Mutton Island, has an area of 210 acres, and consists of excellent pasture-ground.

ENNISKILLEN, a market-town and borough of E., the cap. of the co. of Fermanagh, chiefly in the p. of Enniskillen, but partly in that of Rossory, in co. Fermanagh, 6 m. WNW of Maguire's-Bridge, and 80 m. NW by N of Dublin. The main body of the town stands on an island in the Erne, and is all in the p. of Enniskillen; a suburb on the E is also in the p. of Enniskillen; and a suburb on the W is in the p. of Rossory. The island on which the main body of the town is situated lies about 1 m. above the debouch of the river into the Lower Erne, and has the appearance of a flattened, edificed, stupendous earthwork, or huge oblong mound, surrounded with a great fosse. The island is connected with the two suburbs by two bridges, and measures NW, from bridge to bridge, about 850 yds. The entire appearance of the town, including the arrangement of the streets, the character of the houses, and the opulence of the shops, is greatly superior to that of the majority of Irish towns. The Royal school, founded by Charles I., occupies a conspicuous site in the vicinity of the town, and is one of the most richly endowed in the kingdom. A large infantry barrack occupies the downward tongue or N peninsula of the island; there is also a small artillery barrack; and respectively 200 yds. N of the upper bridge and 150 yds. W of the lower one, are two small forts.—The trade of E. is active. From 300 to 400 pieces of linen are sold at each fortnightly market; and thrice the quantity, it is said, would find purchasers. A considerable number of females in the environs are employed in the manufacture of straw-plait. A considerable export of live cattle and pigs is conducted to Derry. Some of the grain bought in the markets is sent to Derry, Armagh, and other places; but the greater part is locally consumed. Timber, coals, and other sea-borne commodities, are imported by barges from Belleek. A railway is projected from Enniskillen, by Maguire's-Bridge, Lisnaskea, Clones, and Monaghan, to join the Dublin and Belfast junction railway at Newry, and the Ulster railway at Armagh. It has also been proposed to connect Londonderry and Enniskillen by railway. The income of the corporation, derived from rents, tolls, and customs, III. Area of the Rossory section of the town, 69 acres; of the Magheraboy-Enniskillen section, 54 acres; of the Tyrkennedy-Enniskillen section, 87 acres. Pop. of the borough, in 1831, 6,116. Pop. of the town, in 1831, 6,056; in 1841, 5,686. The only building which existed on the site of the town, previous to the plantation of Ulster, was a small fortalice of the Maguires. So rapidly did the town rise under the encouragements of its founder, Sir William Cole, that, in 1641, it covered the greater part of the island, and formed an important asylum and protection for the Protestants. The Enniskilleners are famed for the conspicuous and decisive part which they acted in the war of the Revolution. The town gives the title of Earl, in the peerage of Ireland, to the family of Cole, its chief proprietors.
